fcn.q: A set of rules, which enable orderly exchange of information between two devices.
fcn.expl: A protocol is a set of rules governing communication between devices. Topology refers to network layout, not rules for exchange.
fcn.takeaway: Protocol = rules for orderly data exchange between devices

fcn.q: Proposal for a new Internet standard is called
fcn.expl: Internet drafts are proposals for new Internet standards submitted to the IETF. RFC is a finalized standard document. Draft Standard and Proposed Standard are stages after Internet draft.
fcn.takeaway: New Internet standard proposal = Internet draft (submitted to IETF)

fcn.q: The data sent between layers is called
fcn.expl: PDU (Protocol Data Unit) is the term for data exchanged between peer layers in the OSI model. Datagram is a Network layer PDU specifically.
fcn.takeaway: Data between layers = Protocol Data Unit (PDU)

fcn.q: Topology, which connects every single node in the network to every other node, is
fcn.expl: A PABX (Private Automatic Branch Exchange) uses a star topology where all phones connect to a central switching unit.
fcn.takeaway: PABX = Star topology (central switch connects all extensions)

fcn.q: Which of the following is not a hybrid topology
fcn.expl: Mesh topology connects every node directly to every other node, providing maximum redundancy. Star connects to central hub, Ring forms a circle, Bus uses a shared line.
fcn.takeaway: Full mesh = every node connected to every other node

fcn.q: The term 10Base2 indicates a network
opts: 10 mbps speed, Base Band Signaling, 200 meters segment length
10 mbps speed, Base Band Signaling, 200 meters maximum length
10 mbps speed, Base Band Signaling, 185 meters segment length
fcn.expl: String is not a standard hybrid topology. Tree, Bus, and Star are standard topologies. Hybrid topologies include combinations like Star-Bus.
fcn.takeaway: String is NOT a recognized hybrid topology

fcn.q: Coaxial cable, UTP cable, STP cable and fibre cables are popular network media today. Order these in increasing order of data security
fcn.expl: 10Base2 (Thinnet) operates at 10 Mbps using baseband signaling with a maximum segment length of 185 meters (not 200). The '2' was a rounded value.
fcn.takeaway: 10Base2 = 10Mbps, Baseband, 185m max segment (Thinnet)

fcn.q: Bridges are simple devices that are used to connect LANs of
opts: Different Architecture (eg. Ethernet to Token Ring)
fcn.expl: In terms of data security, UTP is least secure (easily tapped), followed by Coaxial, then STP, with Fiber optic being most secure (hardest to tap).
fcn.takeaway: Security order (least→most): UTP < Coaxial < STP < Fiber

fcn.q: Which application uses existing CSMA/VD over existing twisted-pair cable with bandwidths of 100 mbps?
fcn.expl: Bridges operate at the Data Link layer and connect LANs of the same architecture (e.g., Ethernet to Ethernet). Routers connect different architecture networks.
fcn.takeaway: Bridges = same architecture LANs (Data Link layer device)

fcn.q: What model divides the network communication process into seven layers?
fcn.expl: 100VG-AnyLAN (IEEE 802.12) uses Demand Priority access method over existing 4-pair UTP at 100 Mbps. It was designed to work over voice-grade cabling.
fcn.takeaway: 100VG-AnyLAN = 100Mbps over existing UTP using CSMA/CD variant

fcn.q: B-ISDN uses fiber as a transmission medium and _______ as the switching infrastructure
fcn.expl: The OSI model divides network communication into 7 layers. TCP/IP has 4 layers. X.25 is a WAN protocol, not a model.
fcn.takeaway: OSI = 7-layer network communication model

fcn.q: ______ is the only standards based technology that has been designed from the beginning to accommodate the simultaneous transmission of voice, video and data
fcn.expl: B-ISDN (Broadband ISDN) uses fiber optic transmission and ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode) as its switching technology.
fcn.takeaway: B-ISDN = Fiber transmission + ATM switching infrastructure

fcn.q: Which of the following common LAN protocols extend to OSI network layer.
fcn.expl: ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode) was designed from the start to carry voice, video, and data simultaneously using fixed-size 53-byte cells.
fcn.takeaway: ATM = supports simultaneous voice + video + data from design

fcn.q: Portability standards are discussed widely in four areas, which of the following is not one of them.
fcn.expl: TCP/IP extends to the Network layer. NetBIOS and NetBEUI are limited to lower layers and do not have native network-layer addressing.
fcn.takeaway: TCP/IP extends to OSI Network layer (Layer 3)

fcn.q: Which of the following is not an essential feature of Data Link Layer protocols as defined by ISO/OSI model
fcn.expl: Portability is discussed in OS, data management, programming languages, and user interface areas. Hardware is not a portability concern as software runs on hardware.
fcn.takeaway: Hardware is NOT a portability standard area

fcn.q: Which of the following describes the OSI Class 3 transport (TP3)
fcn.expl: Message orientation is not an essential ISO/OSI Data Link feature. Essential features are error detection, error correction/retransmission, and framing.
fcn.takeaway: Message orientation is NOT a Data Link Layer requirement

fcn.q: The issue of chekpointing and synchronization is handled by which layer
fcn.expl: OSI Transport Class 3 (TP3) provides both error recovery (signaled error recovery) and multiplexing capabilities.
fcn.takeaway: TP3 = Signaled error recovery + multiplexing

fcn.q: In the OSI model, the responsibility for negotiating the encodings to be used in any perticular connection is entrusted upon
fcn.expl: Session layer handles dialog control, checkpointing, and synchronization. It allows sessions to be checkpointed and restarted.
fcn.takeaway: Checkpointing/synchronization = Session Layer (Layer 5)

fcn.q: TCP/IP is built on _________ technology
fcn.expl: The Presentation layer is responsible for data encoding, encryption, compression, and negotiating the format/encoding used during a connection.
fcn.takeaway: Presentation Layer = handles data encoding negotiation

fcn.q: Which of the following is not a network operating system
fcn.expl: TCP/IP is a connectionless (datagram-based) network architecture. Each packet is routed independently. It's not connection-oriented at the IP level.
fcn.takeaway: TCP/IP = built on connectionless datagram technology (IP)

fcn.q: Fiber optic token ring networks operate at the speed of
fcn.expl: Windows 95 is a desktop OS, not a network operating system. Windows for Workgroups, Novell NetWare are NOS products.
fcn.takeaway: Windows 95 = desktop OS, NOT a Network Operating System

fcn.q: The Synchronous Optical Network is an ANSI standard. A similar standard established in Europe is
fcn.expl: Fiber Distributed Data Interface (FDDI) and fiber optic token ring operate at 100 Mbps over fiber. Basic token ring is 4 or 16 Mbps.
fcn.takeaway: Fiber optic token ring (FDDI) = 100 Mbps

fcn.q: In the TCP/IP protocol family ___________ provides reliable transport service.
fcn.expl: SDH (Synchronous Digital Hierarchy) is the European equivalent of SONET (Synchronous Optical Network), standardized by ITU-T.
fcn.takeaway: SONET European equivalent = SDH (Synchronous Digital Hierarchy)

fcn.q: IP address is a ___________ address.
fcn.expl: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) provides reliable, connection-oriented transport service in the TCP/IP stack.
fcn.takeaway: Reliable TCP/IP transport = TCP (connection-oriented, acknowledged)

fcn.q: An IP address when logically ANDed with netmask, the result is _________________.
fcn.expl: An IP address is a Network Layer (Layer 3) logical address, not a hardware/MAC address (Layer 2).
fcn.takeaway: IP address = Network Layer address (Layer 3, logical)

fcn.q: Remote boot uses _______ protocol to discover IP address of diskless machine.
fcn.expl: ANDing an IP address with its subnet mask extracts the network address portion, used for routing decisions.
fcn.takeaway: IP AND Subnet Mask = Network Address

fcn.q: Once a datagram is fragmented in a IP network it is reassembled only at ____________.
fcn.expl: RARP (Reverse ARP) allows a diskless machine to discover its IP address given its MAC address. Used for remote boot/diskless workstations.
fcn.takeaway: Remote boot IP discovery = RARP (Reverse ARP)

fcn.q: The source quench _________ message is used to control the rate at which datagrams are transmitted.
fcn.expl: IP fragmentation: fragments are NOT reassembled at intermediate routers. Reassembly happens only at the final destination to reduce router overhead.
fcn.takeaway: IP fragments reassembled ONLY at final destination

fcn.q: TCP establishes an end to end _______________ between the sender and receiver.
fcn.expl: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) carries the source quench message, which tells the sender to slow down transmission rate.
fcn.takeaway: Source quench = ICMP message (congestion control signal)

fcn.q: UDP is used with ___________________ Protocol.
fcn.expl: TCP establishes a virtual circuit (logical connection) via 3-way handshake before data transfer begins.
fcn.takeaway: TCP = establishes virtual circuit (connection-oriented)

fcn.q: IP is a ___________ protocol
fcn.expl: UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is used with TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol) because TFTP is lightweight and handles its own basic error checking.
fcn.takeaway: UDP used with TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol)

fcn.q: ______ is a complex data structure that contains details about a connection
fcn.expl: IP is a routed protocol - it is the protocol that gets routed (carries data). Routing protocols (RIP, OSPF) are used by routers to route IP.
fcn.takeaway: IP = routed protocol (carries data; routing protocols route it)

fcn.q: The bind socket call is used to __________________________
opts: Bind a local application to a remote application
Bind a socket to local port
Bind a socket to a remote port
Bind a local port to a remote port
fcn.expl: TCB (Transmission Control Block) is a complex data structure maintained by TCP that stores all connection state information.
fcn.takeaway: TCB (Transmission Control Block) = TCP connection data structure

fcn.q: Each SNMP managed object belongs to a ______________
fcn.expl: The bind() system call associates a socket with a specific local port number and address on the host.
fcn.takeaway: bind() socket call = associates socket with a local port

fcn.q: RTCP is the ________________ protocol designed to work with RTP
fcn.expl: SNMP organizes managed objects into communities for access control. A community acts as a password/group for SNMP access.
fcn.takeaway: SNMP managed objects belong to a Community

fcn.q: For block devices all I/O occurs through the _________________
fcn.expl: RTCP (RTP Control Protocol) is the control protocol that works alongside RTP to provide QoS feedback and synchronization.
fcn.takeaway: RTCP = Control protocol for RTP (feedback/sync)

fcn.q: _______ is a international standard file format for describing interactive 3D multimedia on the Internet.
fcn.expl: In Unix/Linux, all block device I/O goes through the buffer cache (also called page cache), which improves performance by caching disk blocks.
fcn.takeaway: Block device I/O = goes through buffer cache

fcn.q: ___________ is a distance-vector routing protocol
fcn.expl: VRML (Virtual Reality Markup Language) is the international standard for describing interactive 3D multimedia on the Internet.
fcn.takeaway: VRML = standard for interactive 3D multimedia on Internet

fcn.q: IPng provides security through Authentication Headers and the default encryption method is ________
fcn.expl: RIP (Routing Information Protocol) is a distance-vector protocol. OSPF is link-state. IGRP is also distance-vector but Cisco proprietary.
fcn.takeaway: RIP = distance-vector routing protocol

fcn.q: RTSP is a ___________ protocol similar to HTTP
fcn.expl: IPng (IPv6) Authentication Headers provide security. The default encryption method used is MD5 for integrity verification.
fcn.takeaway: IPng/IPv6 default encryption = MD5

fcn.q: OSPF features include __________________.
opts: Routing based on upper-layer TOS request
fcn.expl: RTSP (Real-Time Streaming Protocol) is an Application layer protocol similar to HTTP, used for controlling streaming media servers.
fcn.takeaway: RTSP = Application layer streaming control protocol (like HTTP)

fcn.q: IP address is assigned to a __________.
fcn.expl: OSPF supports multi-path routing, equal-cost multi-path (ECMP), and TOS-based routing. All listed features are valid OSPF features.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF features: Multi-path + Equal-cost + TOS-based routing

fcn.q: Token Ring network operate at a speed of
fcn.expl: An IP address is assigned to a specific network interface (NIC), not to the host itself or the network. One host can have multiple IPs if it has multiple interfaces.
fcn.takeaway: IP address assigned to an interface (not host or network)

fcn.q: Ethernet is an access method that strictly adheres to the
fcn.expl: Standard Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) operates at 4 Mbps or 16 Mbps. The question asks 'a speed of' - 16 Mbps is the common/faster standard.
fcn.takeaway: Token Ring = 4 or 16 Mbps (IEEE 802.5)

fcn.q: 100BASET4 operates at 100 mbps using base band signaling and the media is
opts: Two pairs of data grade UTP
Four pairs of telephone grade UTP
fcn.expl: Ethernet uses CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) as its media access control method.
fcn.takeaway: Ethernet = CSMA/CD access method (IEEE 802.3)

fcn.q: Length of Ethernet address is
fcn.expl: 100BASE-T4 uses four pairs of telephone-grade (Category 3) UTP cable to achieve 100 Mbps by splitting data across the pairs.
fcn.takeaway: 100BASE-T4 = 4 pairs of telephone-grade UTP cable

fcn.q: Frame Relay protocol is used for
fcn.expl: Ethernet/MAC addresses are 48 bits (6 bytes) long, expressed as 12 hexadecimal digits.
fcn.takeaway: Ethernet (MAC) address = 48 bits (6 bytes)

fcn.q: Distance of a Radio Link is limited by
fcn.expl: Frame Relay is a WAN (Wide Area Network) protocol providing virtual circuit connections over packet-switched networks.
fcn.takeaway: Frame Relay = WAN protocol (virtual circuits)

fcn.q: Basic Rate ISDN service provides
fcn.expl: Radio link distance is limited by line of sight. Physical obstacles and the curvature of the Earth limit direct RF propagation.
fcn.takeaway: Radio link = limited by line of sight distance

fcn.q: X.25 networks work at the maximum speed of
fcn.expl: Basic Rate ISDN (BRI) provides 2 Bearer channels (64 Kbps each) and 1 Delta channel (16 Kbps) = 2B+1D.
fcn.takeaway: Basic Rate ISDN = 2B+1D (2×64Kbps + 16Kbps)

fcn.q: Unit for data at Transport layer is
fcn.expl: X.25 packet-switched networks support data rates up to 64 Kbps on each virtual circuit.
fcn.takeaway: X.25 max speed = 64 Kbps

fcn.q: A physical layer address of a node is
opts: Changes if the NIC hardware is changed
Assigned by the administrator of the node
fcn.expl: At the Transport layer (Layer 4), the PDU is called a Segment (TCP) or Datagram (UDP). 'Segment' is the primary term.
fcn.takeaway: Transport layer PDU = Segment

fcn.q: Path determination occurs at
fcn.expl: A physical layer address (MAC address) is burned into the NIC hardware. If you replace the NIC, the MAC address changes.
fcn.takeaway: Physical address = changes if NIC hardware changes

fcn.q: One of the following is not a function of Transport Layer.
fcn.expl: Path determination (routing) occurs at the Network layer (Layer 3). Routers operate at this layer to determine best paths.
fcn.takeaway: Path determination = Network Layer (Layer 3)

fcn.q: Network layer communicates path information using
opts: Some form of routing information protocol
fcn.expl: Addressing is not a Transport layer function. Addressing is handled by Network layer (IP). Transport does windowing, multiplexing, and flow control.
fcn.takeaway: Addressing is NOT a Transport layer function (it's Network layer)

fcn.q: Applications running on a single host are identified by service access points at Transport layer and Session layer interface. These SAPs are known as
fcn.expl: The Network layer communicates path information using routing information protocols (RIP, OSPF, BGP, etc.).
fcn.takeaway: Network layer path info = routing information protocols (RIP/OSPF)

fcn.q: Route poisoning technique is used to avoid
fcn.expl: SAPs (Service Access Points) at the Transport/Session interface are called ports (TCP/UDP port numbers) and together with IP form sockets.
fcn.takeaway: Transport layer SAPs = Ports (TCP/UDP port numbers)

fcn.q: The Transport Layer provide
opts: Best effort end to end packet delivery service
Connection oriented end to end packet delivery service
Connection oriented, reliable, end to end packet delivery service
fcn.expl: Route poisoning (setting metric to infinity) prevents count-to-infinity problems in distance-vector routing protocols like RIP.
fcn.takeaway: Route poisoning = prevents count-to-infinity problem

fcn.q: Application data is converted in a form suitable for transmission on the network by
fcn.expl: The Transport layer provides connection-oriented, reliable, end-to-end packet delivery (TCP). Best-effort is IP (Network layer).
fcn.takeaway: Transport layer = connection-oriented, reliable, end-to-end delivery

fcn.q: Version 4 Internet Protocol uses
fcn.expl: The Presentation layer converts application data into a format suitable for transmission, handling encoding, encryption, and compression.
fcn.takeaway: Presentation Layer = converts app data for transmission

fcn.q: Mapping between Internet Protocol address and MAC layer address are resolved using
fcn.expl: IPv4 uses a 32-bit hierarchical address scheme divided into network and host portions.
fcn.takeaway: IPv4 = 32-bit hierarchical address scheme

fcn.q: UDP is faster than TCP because
fcn.expl: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) resolves IP addresses to MAC layer addresses. RARP does the reverse.
fcn.takeaway: IP to MAC mapping = ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)

fcn.q: Digital Signatures use
fcn.expl: UDP is faster than TCP because it provides no reliability mechanisms (no acknowledgments, sequencing, or retransmission).
fcn.takeaway: UDP faster = no reliability (no ACKs, no retransmission)

fcn.q: Domain Name Service provides an easy and fast mechanism to extract
opts: Network address from an IP address
IP address corresponding to a host name
MAC layer address corresponding to a given IP address
fcn.expl: Digital signatures use public key (asymmetric) cryptography. The sender signs with private key, receiver verifies with public key.
fcn.takeaway: Digital signatures = Public Key Algorithm (asymmetric crypto)

fcn.q: Most popular transfer agent to transfer electronic mail on Internet is
fcn.expl: DNS (Domain Name Service) maps host names to IP addresses. It does not map to MAC addresses (that's ARP).
fcn.takeaway: DNS = resolves host name to IP address

fcn.q: Real life Token Ring Networks use
fcn.expl: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is the most widely used mail transfer agent for sending email on the Internet.
fcn.takeaway: Most popular Internet email transfer = SMTP

fcn.q: TCP provides connection oriented service on top of ....
fcn.expl: Real-life Token Ring uses a star-wired ring physical topology. The wiring looks like a star but logically forms a ring.
fcn.takeaway: Token Ring physical = star-wired ring topology

fcn.q: Ethernet address is ... bits wide.
fcn.expl: TCP provides connection-oriented service over a connectionless (packet-switched/datagram) network infrastructure.
fcn.takeaway: TCP connection-oriented over connectionless packet-switched network

fcn.q: The network model in which only two hosts are connected to each other is called...
fcn.expl: Ethernet MAC addresses are 48 bits wide (6 bytes), shown as 12 hexadecimal characters.
fcn.takeaway: Ethernet address = 48 bits wide

fcn.q: A Private Automatic Branch Exchange (APBX) is an example of .....
fcn.expl: A network with only two hosts directly connected is called a point-to-point network.
fcn.takeaway: Two hosts connected directly = Point-to-Point network

fcn.q: The protocol used for discovering Ethernet address corresponding to an IP address is
fcn.expl: PABX (Private Automatic Branch Exchange) uses star topology with the PBX as the central switch.
fcn.takeaway: PABX = Star Topology

fcn.q: T-Connectors are used in ..... networks.
fcn.expl: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) discovers the Ethernet (MAC) address corresponding to a given IP address via broadcast.
fcn.takeaway: IP to Ethernet address = ARP (broadcast-based discovery)

fcn.q: __________Technology requires proper termination of segments.
fcn.expl: T-connectors are used in 10Base2 (Thinnet coaxial) networks to connect the NIC to the coaxial bus cable.
fcn.takeaway: T-connectors = Coaxial (10Base2 Thinnet) networks

fcn.q: ____________ is not a responsibility of OSI Presentation layer
fcn.expl: 10Base2 (Thinnet coaxial) requires proper termination at both ends of the bus segment to prevent signal reflections.
fcn.takeaway: 10Base2 requires proper termination (coaxial bus)

fcn.q: ____________ layer of OSI does not have a corresponding layer in TCP/IP stack.
fcn.expl: The Presentation layer handles data compression, encryption, and translation. Data Recovery is NOT a Presentation layer function.
fcn.takeaway: Data Recovery is NOT an OSI Presentation layer responsibility

fcn.q: HTTP is a __________ application layer protocol.
fcn.expl: The Session layer of OSI has no direct equivalent in the 4-layer TCP/IP model. Session functions are handled by the Application layer in TCP/IP.
fcn.takeaway: Session Layer = no TCP/IP equivalent (absorbed into Application)

fcn.q: What Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) layer does IP belong to?
fcn.expl: HTTP is a stateless protocol - each request is independent with no memory of previous requests. This requires cookies/sessions for state management.
fcn.takeaway: HTTP = stateless application layer protocol

fcn.q: Which of the following IP addresses is the loopback address?
fcn.expl: IP (Internet Protocol) operates at the Network layer (Layer 3) of the OSI model.
fcn.takeaway: IP = Network Layer (Layer 3) of OSI

fcn.q: Personal computers configured with more than one IP address are called
fcn.expl: 127.0.0.1 is the loopback address (also written as localhost). 0.0.0.0 means 'this host', 255.255.255.255 is broadcast.
fcn.takeaway: Loopback address = 127.0.0.1

fcn.q: Which utility program reports whether a networked computer is responding at a given IP address?
fcn.expl: A computer with more than one IP address (multiple network interfaces or virtual interfaces) is called a multihomed host.
fcn.takeaway: Multiple IP addresses = multihomed host

fcn.q: Can IP run over any other physical networks besides Ethernet?
opts: No (and IP does not run over Ethernet)
Yes (but IP does not run over Ethernet)
fcn.expl: Ping (Packet Internet Groper) reports whether a networked computer is reachable and measures round-trip time.
fcn.takeaway: Ping = tests if networked computer is reachable

fcn.q: What function does Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) perform?
opts: maps IP addresses to Ethernet addresses
automatically assigns IP addresses to computers
prevents two computers from using the same IP address
fcn.expl: IP can run over any physical network technology (Ethernet, Token Ring, ATM, serial links, WiFi, etc.). It is network-layer agnostic.
fcn.takeaway: IP can run over ANY physical network technology

fcn.q: What mechanism does ARP use to resolve IP addresses?
fcn.expl: ARP resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses. It sends a broadcast asking 'Who has this IP address? Tell me your MAC.'
fcn.takeaway: ARP = resolves IP address to MAC (hardware) address

fcn.q: Which of the following services manage the conversion between IP addresses and host names?
fcn.expl: ARP uses broadcasting to send a request to all devices on the network segment to find the MAC address for a given IP.
fcn.takeaway: ARP resolution mechanism = broadcasting

fcn.q: The IP checksum feature supports integrity checking for
opts: none of the above - IP doesn’t use checksums
fcn.expl: DNS (Domain Name System) and WINS manage IP-to-hostname conversion. NIS is Unix-specific. DNS is the standard service.
fcn.takeaway: DNS manages IP address to hostname conversion

fcn.q: The IP delivery service is
fcn.expl: IP checksum covers only the IP header fields, NOT the payload/data. Upper-layer protocols (TCP/UDP) provide their own checksums for data.
fcn.takeaway: IP checksum = covers IP header ONLY (not payload)

fcn.q: UDP ________ allow different applications to maintain their own "channels" for data
fcn.expl: IP delivery is unreliable and connectionless (best effort). It does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or error-free transmission.
fcn.takeaway: IP delivery = unreliable, connectionless (best effort)

fcn.q: The UDP header consists of four (4) fields of two bytes each: ________ is not one of them
fcn.expl: UDP uses port numbers to allow different applications on the same host to communicate simultaneously using multiplexing.
fcn.takeaway: UDP ports = allow multiple applications to communicate (multiplexing)

fcn.q: ______ allow applications to communicate using standard mechanisms built into network hardware and operating systems.
fcn.expl: The UDP header contains 4 fields: source port, destination port, length, and checksum. Each field is 2 bytes (16 bits).
fcn.takeaway: UDP header = 4 fields × 2 bytes: src port, dst port, length, checksum

fcn.q: A host has an IP address of 192.168.6.209 and a net mask of 255.255.255.0. Which one of the following statements is true?
opts: The subnet number is 192.168.6.0, and the host number is 209.
The subnet number is 192.168.6.192, and the host number is 17.
The subnet number is 192.168.6.200, and the host number is 9.
The subnet number is 192.168.6.208, and the host number is 1.
fcn.expl: Sockets (Berkeley socket API) allow applications to communicate over a network using standard mechanisms (IP+port combination).
fcn.takeaway: Sockets = allow applications to communicate via standard mechanisms

fcn.q: Which of the following protocols is used for network management and monitoring?
fcn.takeaway: Subnet calculation: IP AND mask gives network address

fcn.q: Your network segment has a default gateway of 205.222.45.126 and a subnet mask of 255.255.255.192. Which of the following is true?
opts: The valid range for hosts is 205.222.1.126 through 205.222.44.126 and all the hosts must use the same subnet mask.
The valid range for hosts is 205.222.45.126 through 205.222.45.126 and all the hosts must increase the subnet mask by one.
The valid range for hosts is 205.222.45.65 through 205.222.45.125 and all the hosts must use the same subnet mask.
The valid range for hosts is 205.222.1.65 through 205.222.45.126 and all the hosts must use the same subnet mask.
fcn.expl: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) is used for network management, monitoring devices, and collecting network statistics.
fcn.takeaway: Network management protocol = SNMP

fcn.q: When developers write TCP/IP software, they have two mechanisms for transporting data from applications: TCP and UDP. Why, in many cases, is UDP used as the protocol for communications?
opts: UDP has a smaller packet size, and thus can be sent around the network with more speed.
TCP has no mechanism to support short, choppy bursts of data, which some applications tend to produce.
UDP is never used for applications. It is simply a routing protocol.
UDP provides reliable delivery acknowledgements, which many applications need for success.
fcn.expl: 205.222.45.126 with a /26 mask means the subnet has 64 addresses (.64-.127). .126 is the broadcast address of that subnet.
fcn.takeaway: Default gateway on /26 subnet: identify network range

fcn.q: In a token-passing network, what can each and every device on the network be described as behaving like?
fcn.expl: TCP/IP software developers can use sockets (Berkeley socket API) or TLI (Transport Layer Interface) to implement networking.
fcn.takeaway: TCP/IP software: two APIs = Sockets or TLI

fcn.q: You have become increasingly concerned with the activity at the Logical Link Layer of the IPX/SPX stack on a certain workstation. What data form should you be concentrating on at this level?
fcn.expl: In token-passing networks, a device can only transmit when it holds the token. Each device can transmit for a limited time.
fcn.takeaway: Token ring: device transmits ONLY when holding the token

fcn.q: TCP/IP Protocol Architecture was designed to carry data over the ARPANET. What type of network is the ARPANET?
opts: A hybrid of a packet and message routing network.
fcn.expl: A protocol analyzer (packet sniffer) captures and analyzes network traffic, allowing monitoring of network activity.
fcn.takeaway: Monitoring network activity = protocol analyzer/packet sniffer

fcn.q: Your company has been assigned a Class B IP address. You are administrating a country-wide network with 45 individual subnets. Over the next year, you will be adding 60 subnets. What subnet mask should you use that will allow the most hosts per subnet?
fcn.expl: TCP/IP was designed to carry data over large, heterogeneous networks with different underlying technologies.
fcn.takeaway: TCP/IP designed for large heterogeneous networks

fcn.q: What layer of the OSI model handles such issues as file access and transfer and virtual terminal emulation?
fcn.expl: Class B gives 16 bits for hosts. With specific subnetting requirements, calculate needed bits.
fcn.takeaway: Class B subnetting: 16 host bits available for subnet/host division

fcn.q: You have implemented RSA encryption technology on your network. It seems to be working well. However, one station always receives encrypted emails as garbled text, meaning the protocol stack is corrupted. What OSI layer above can be assumed to be faulty?
fcn.expl: The Application layer (Layer 7) handles file access, transfer, and management, as well as other application services.
fcn.takeaway: File access and management = Application Layer (Layer 7)

fcn.q: What is the maximum segment length for 10Broad36?
fcn.expl: RSA encryption provides asymmetric (public key) encryption for secure key exchange and digital signatures.
fcn.takeaway: RSA = asymmetric public key encryption technology

fcn.q: Which networking standard describes cabling requirements for Ethernet?
fcn.expl: 10Broad36 is a broadband coaxial specification with a maximum segment length of 3600 meters (36 × 100m).
fcn.takeaway: 10Broad36 max segment = 3600 meters

fcn.q: You have been given the Class C address of 205.222.5.0. What size subnet mask will give you the greatest possible number of hosts?
fcn.expl: IEEE 802.3 defines the Ethernet standard including cabling requirements, speeds, and access methods.
fcn.takeaway: Ethernet cabling standard = IEEE 802.3

fcn.q: Describe a fully-meshed star network.
opts: A network with a central router that has a unique path to each end-point router and some of the end-point routers have links to one another.
A network with a central router that feeds a number of smaller routers that in turn feed a number of end-point routers.
A network with a central router that has a unique path to each end-point router.
A network in which all the routers have unique paths to one another
fcn.expl: Class C 205.222.5.0: default mask is /24 (255.255.255.0). An 8-bit mask from host bits for subnetting gives 255.255.255.0 for 254 hosts.
fcn.takeaway: Class C default = 255.255.255.0 providing 254 host addresses

fcn.q: Why is SNMP considered lacking in security?
opts: SNMP devices send messages about their failing conditions.
The SNMP manager broadcasts sensitive information to the managed devices.
The SNMP passwords are clear ASCII text.
fcn.expl: A fully-meshed star network has a central node connected to all others, AND all peripheral nodes also directly connected to each other.
fcn.takeaway: Fully-meshed star = central hub + all nodes directly connected

fcn.q: Which one of the following statements correctly describes differences between a bridge and a layer two switch?
opts: A layer two switch is a high speed, multi-port bridge.
A layer two switch cannot use the Spanning Tree Protocol to learn a network’s topology, while a bridge can.
A layer two switch can support multiple network media types, such as twisted pair, fiber, and coax, while a bridge is limited to supporting a single network media type.
A layer two switch is than an Ethernet hub that supports different speeds on different ports, while a bridge requires all ports to have the same speed.
fcn.expl: SNMP v1/v2 uses community strings (plain-text passwords) for authentication, providing very weak security with no encryption.
fcn.takeaway: SNMP lacks security: uses plain-text community strings

fcn.q: How are packet forwarding decisions made in an OSI reference model layer three device?
opts: The device compares the destination MAC address to an internal table that associates MAC addresses to particular ports.
In a layer three device, every packet is sent out of every port.
In a layer three device, packets are forwarded based on node name.
The device compares the destination network address to an internal table that associates network addresses to particular ports. 111.Which one of the following describes the IP address 192.4.2.4?
fcn.expl: Distance vector routing works by sharing routing tables with neighbors; routers only know distance/direction to destinations.
fcn.takeaway: Distance vector: routers share routing tables with neighbors

fcn.q: What is the main function of a router?
opts: Setting up communications between networks that use dissimilar IP segments but the same Subnet Masks
Providing IP configuration to computers using DHCP
fcn.expl: In OSI, packet forwarding uses Layer 3 (Network layer) addresses (IP addresses) to make routing decisions.
fcn.takeaway: OSI packet forwarding = based on Layer 3 (Network) addresses

fcn.q: Network cards in computers communicate with each other using unique MAC addresses, similar to street addresses. Where do these unique addresses come from?
opts: The network administrator makes up addresses to use and then programs them into the software.
The manufacturer publishes a range of addresses in the manual that you choose from.
The network card searches the network and then assigns itself a unique address from an elaborate algorithm using the alread-allocated addresses.
The IEEE assigns every manufacturer a prefix and a block of address range, typically expressed in hexadecimal notation. Answers the followings in brief:
fcn.expl: A router's main function is to forward (route) packets between different networks using IP addresses and routing tables.
fcn.takeaway: Router main function = route packets between networks (Layer 3)

fcn.q: What does it mean if a protocol is stateless?
opts: If a stateless protocol is used, it cannot be traced.
Server responds to a request, and then forgets about it
Client responds to server request and keeps track of the conversation.
If a protocol is stateless, it means it is unreliable
fcn.expl: A stateless protocol means the server processes each request independently and retains no memory of past interactions. After responding, the server forgets about it. HTTP is a classic example.
fcn.takeaway: Stateless protocol = server responds then forgets (no session memory)

fcn.q: Identify the FALSE statement about Wireless LAN and Ethernet.
opts: Wireless LAN and Ethernet are broadcast medium
WLAN provides less bandwidth compared to Gigabit Ethernet
WLAN & Ethernet can use IP as their network layer protocol
fcn.expl: WLAN and Ethernet CAN co-exist — many networks use both simultaneously. Devices can connect via either Wi-Fi or wired Ethernet to the same network infrastructure.
fcn.takeaway: FALSE: WLAN and Ethernet CANNOT co-exist — they absolutely CAN co-exist

fcn.q: Which of the following statement about ICMP is FALSE?
opts: ICMP sends 5 types of error reporting messages and 4 pairs of query messages to support unreliable and connectionless IP
The source-quench error message is sent in an effort to alleviate congestion
The echo-request and echo-reply messages test the connectivity between 2 systems
ICMP messages are not encapsulated in IP datagram
fcn.expl: ICMP messages ARE encapsulated within IP datagrams — this statement is FALSE. ICMP uses IP as its transport. Options A, B, C are all true statements about ICMP.
fcn.takeaway: FALSE: ICMP messages ARE encapsulated in IP datagrams

fcn.q: Which of the following is not part of the IP header?
fcn.expl: Window size is a TCP (Transport layer) header field, NOT an IP header field. Fragment offset, Header checksum, and TTL are all standard IPv4 header fields.
fcn.takeaway: Window size = TCP header field, NOT IP header

fcn.q: Which of these commands displays the IP address and MAC address mappings known to a Windows system?
fcn.expl: The 'arp -a' command displays the ARP cache — the table of IP-to-MAC address mappings the system has learned. Ipconfig shows IP config, Netstat shows connections, Tracert traces routes.
fcn.takeaway: arp -a = displays IP-to-MAC address mapping table (ARP cache)

fcn.q: Match the following with the corresponding protocols: IP to host name resolution, Accessing mail account, Who owns IP address 172.16.1.2?, Destination unreachable
fcn.expl: IP to hostname = DNS (iii), Accessing mail = IMAP (iv), Who owns IP? = ARP (ii), Destination unreachable = ICMP (i). Correct match: a-iii, b-iv, c-ii, d-i.
fcn.takeaway: DNS=name resolution, IMAP=mail, ARP=IP owner lookup, ICMP=error messages

fcn.q: Which of the following statements is a true description of Fiber Optic Cable?
opts: Fiber optic cables transfer data in a mesh
Fiber optic cables are similar to coaxial cables
Fiber optic cables transfer data in light form
Fiber optic cables are similar to twisted pair cables
fcn.expl: Fiber optic cables transmit data as pulses of light through glass or plastic fiber strands. This makes them immune to electromagnetic interference and capable of very high speeds over long distances.
fcn.takeaway: Fiber optic = transfers data as light pulses (immune to EMI)

fcn.q: What is the maximum data field length (MTU) supported by Ethernet?
fcn.expl: The Ethernet MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) for the data/payload field is 1500 bytes. The total frame size including header and FCS is 1518 bytes, but the MTU refers to the data field only.
fcn.takeaway: Ethernet MTU (data field) = 1500 bytes; total frame = 1518 bytes

fcn.q: For which of the following is UDP unsuitable? Choose the most appropriate answer.
fcn.expl: SMTP (email) requires reliable, ordered delivery — it uses TCP. DNS, RTP, and multicasting work well with UDP because they tolerate some packet loss and prioritize speed over reliability.
fcn.takeaway: UDP unsuitable for SMTP (email needs reliable TCP); DNS/RTP/multicast use UDP

fcn.q: A router has entries: 192.168.43.0/24 on eth0, 192.168.43.0/25 on eth1, 192.12.17.5/32 on eth3, Default on eth2. On which interface will packets to 192.168.43.16 and 192.168.17.10 be forwarded?
fcn.expl: 192.168.43.16: longest prefix match — /25 mask covers .0-.127, so eth1 wins. 192.168.17.10: no specific match, uses default route = eth2. Answer: eth1 and eth2.
fcn.takeaway: Longest prefix match wins; unmatched packets use default route

fcn.q: Which of the following uniquely identifies a TCP connection?
opts: Sender IP & Sender Port, Receiver IP & Receiver Port
Sender MAC & Sender IP, Receiver MAC & Receiver IP
fcn.expl: A TCP connection is uniquely identified by a 4-tuple: source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port. This combination (socket pair) uniquely identifies every TCP connection.
fcn.takeaway: TCP connection = 4-tuple: SrcIP + SrcPort + DstIP + DstPort

fcn.q: Which IEEE standard defines Token Ring?
fcn.expl: IEEE 802.5 defines the Token Ring standard. 802.3 is Ethernet, 802.11 is Wi-Fi/WLAN, 802.7 is Broadband LAN.
fcn.takeaway: Token Ring = IEEE 802.5 (Ethernet=802.3, Wi-Fi=802.11)

fcn.q: Which type of packet (or address) is NOT supported in IPv6?
fcn.expl: IPv6 does NOT support broadcast. It replaced broadcast with multicast and anycast. IPv6 supports unicast, multicast, and anycast address types.
fcn.takeaway: IPv6 has NO broadcast — replaced by multicast and anycast

fcn.q: Match the following connectors: UTP, Serial Interface, Telephone with RJ11, RJ45, V.35
opts: UTP-RJ11, Serial I/F – RJ45, Telephone – V.35
UTP-RJ45, Serial I/F – V.35, Telephone – RJ11
UTP-V.35, Serial I/F – RJ45, Telephone – RJ11
UTP-RJ45, Serial I/F – RJ11, Telephone – V.35
fcn.expl: UTP uses RJ45 connectors (8-pin), telephone lines use RJ11 (6-pin), and serial WAN interfaces (like T1/E1) use V.35 connectors.
fcn.takeaway: UTP=RJ45, Telephone=RJ11, Serial WAN interface=V.35

fcn.q: After a host name is resolved to an IP address, what does the local host need to do next to communicate with the target host on the SAME subnet?
opts: Resolve the IP address to physical address using ARP
Resolve the IP address to physical address using RARP
Send the packet to the default Router
fcn.expl: On the same subnet, the host must use ARP to resolve the destination IP to a MAC address before it can send an Ethernet frame directly. No routing is needed since both are on the same network.
fcn.takeaway: Same subnet communication: use ARP to get destination MAC address

fcn.q: A hub connects 24 computers. Computer A sends data to Computer B. How many computers will receive that data?
opts: Only A and B will receive
All computers except A will receive
fcn.expl: A hub is a Layer 1 device that broadcasts all received frames to every port. When A sends data, all 23 other computers (including B) receive it — A does not receive its own transmission.
fcn.takeaway: Hub = broadcasts to ALL ports; all computers except sender receive the frame

fcn.q: Which type of NAT maps a private IP address to a public IP address on a one-to-one basis?
fcn.expl: Static NAT creates a permanent one-to-one mapping between a private IP and a public IP. Dynamic NAT maps from a pool dynamically. PAT maps many private IPs to one public IP using port numbers.
fcn.takeaway: Static NAT = permanent one-to-one private-to-public IP mapping

fcn.q: Which TCP flag is set in the FIRST packet sent from a web client to a web server when opening a website?
fcn.expl: The TCP 3-way handshake begins with the client sending a SYN (synchronize) packet to the server. This is the first step to establish a TCP connection before any HTTP data is transferred.
fcn.takeaway: TCP connection start = client sends SYN (first step of 3-way handshake)

fcn.q: Which of the statements is NOT true related to a network switch?
opts: All the computers connected to a switch are in the same broadcast domain
Each port of a switch is a different broadcast domain
The switch uses source and destination MAC address fields in Ethernet frames to create MAC table
Each port of a switch is a different collision domain
fcn.expl: Each port of a switch is a different collision domain (true), but all ports on a switch are in the SAME broadcast domain (unless VLANs are configured). Option B is false — ports are NOT separate broadcast domains by default.
fcn.takeaway: Switch: each port = separate collision domain, but SAME broadcast domain

fcn.q: Which device stops MAC address broadcasts and thus separates two broadcast domains?
fcn.expl: Routers operate at Layer 3 and do not forward Layer 2 broadcast frames — they separate broadcast domains. Switches forward broadcasts to all ports (same broadcast domain). Hubs and repeaters forward everything.
fcn.takeaway: Router = separates broadcast domains (does not forward L2 broadcasts)

fcn.q: A computer has IP 192.168.100.35 with subnet mask 255.255.255.224 (/27). Which IP address is NOT in the same subnet?
fcn.expl: 255.255.255.224 = /27 (32 addresses per subnet). 192.168.100.35 is in subnet .32-.63 (network .32, broadcast .63). .33, .55, .60 are all in range .32-.63. .65 is in the next subnet (.64-.95) — NOT in same subnet.
fcn.takeaway: /27 subnet of .35 = .32-.63; .65 falls in next subnet (.64-.95)

fcn.q: What is the broadcast address for network 10.10.0.0 with subnet mask 255.255.0.0?
fcn.expl: Broadcast address = network address with all host bits set to 1. For 10.10.0.0/16 (mask 255.255.0.0), the host portion is the last 16 bits. All 1s = 255.255, giving broadcast 10.10.255.255.
fcn.takeaway: Broadcast = all host bits = 1; 10.10.0.0/16 broadcast = 10.10.255.255

fcn.q: What is the correct compressed representation of IPv6 address 3ffe:3700:0200:00ff:0000:0000:0000:0001?
fcn.expl: IPv6 compression: remove leading zeros per group (0200→200, 00ff→ff), replace consecutive all-zero groups with :: (0000:0000:0000→::). Result: 3ffe:3700:200:ff::1. Cannot remove non-leading zeros (3700 stays as 3700).
fcn.takeaway: IPv6 compression: drop leading zeros + use :: for consecutive zero groups

fcn.q: You can connect to 202.141.151.33 but NOT to www.somesite.in (same IP). What is most likely wrong?
opts: The company firewall restricts access to websites
A virus could have caused the problem
The DNS server or DNS settings are not properly configured
The PC is configured to accept only IP addresses
fcn.expl: Since the IP works but the domain name doesn't, the issue is name resolution. The DNS server is not responding or DNS settings are misconfigured. DNS translates the hostname to the IP address.
fcn.takeaway: IP works but domain fails = DNS problem (name resolution broken)

fcn.q: Which OS command shows the routing table of the machine?
opts: Systems do not have routing tables, only routers have routing tables
fcn.expl: The 'netstat -r' command displays the routing table of the local machine. All hosts have a routing table (with at least a default gateway entry). 'arp -all' shows ARP cache, not routing table.
fcn.takeaway: netstat -r = displays the local machine's routing table

fcn.q: Correctly match the Layer Name with the Name of the unit exchanged at that layer (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport).
fcn.expl: OSI layer PDUs: Physical layer = Bit, Data Link layer = Frame, Network layer = Packet, Transport layer = Segment/TPDU. These are the standard names for protocol data units at each layer.
fcn.takeaway: PDUs: Physical=Bit, Data Link=Frame, Network=Packet, Transport=Segment/TPDU

fcn.q: Match the following Devices with the layers it belongs/functionalities? Protocol Functionalities
opts: Get MAC Address for this IP Address i) RARP
Get IP Address for this MAC Address ii) ICMP
Get The Host Name for this IP iii) ARP
Destination is unreachable iv) DNS (a) a- i, b-iii, c-iv, d-ii (b) a- iii, b-i, c-iv, d-ii (c) a- i, b-iii, c-ii, d-iv (d) a- iii, b-i, c-ii, d-iv
fcn.expl: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically assigns IP addresses and other network config to clients.
fcn.takeaway: DHCP = Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (auto IP assignment)

fcn.q: Correctly match the "Layer Name" with the "Name of the unit exchanged" at that Layer.
opts: Transport Layer iv) Frame (a) A - iii, B - iv, C - i, D - ii (b) A- iii, B- i, C-ii, D-iv (c) A- iii, B- iv, C- ii, D- i (d) A-iv, B-iii, C-ii, D-I
fcn.expl: Hub operates at Physical layer; Bridge at Data Link; Router at Network; Switch at Data Link layer.
fcn.takeaway: Hub=Layer1, Bridge=Layer2, Router=Layer3, Switch=Layer2

fcn.q: Which of the following statements is correct with regard to Asymmetric Digital Subscriber Line (ADSL) technology? (A) ADSL has two different data rates, one for incoming and another for outgoing connections. (B) ADSL is a 64 Kbps leased line. (C) ADSL needs special cabling infrastructure. (D) The same domestic telephone connection can not be shared for normal telephony as well as for ADSL. 41-what do WANs do
opts: provide full and part time connectivity
all of the above 42-what is the decimal number 151 in binary?
fcn.expl: Physical=Bit, Data Link=Frame, Network=Packet, Transport=TPDU/Segment. Matching: A-iii, B-iv, C-i, D-ii.
fcn.takeaway: OSI PDUs: Physical=Bit, DataLink=Frame, Network=Packet, Transport=TPDU

fcn.q: how many host addresses are available to a class C network?
fcn.expl: VLAN=802.1q, Ethernet=802.3, WLAN=802.11, Token Bus=802.4, Token Ring=802.5.
fcn.takeaway: IEEE standards: Ethernet=802.3, VLAN=802.1q, WLAN=802.11

fcn.q: how many subnets can a class C network use?
fcn.expl: Subnetting Class C: the default host bits available are 8. Hosts per subnet = 2^n - 2.
fcn.takeaway: Class C: 8 host bits, 254 usable host addresses default

fcn.q: how many bits can be borrowed to created a subnet for a class C network?
fcn.expl: A Class C network has 8 host bits, giving 2^8 - 2 = 254 usable host addresses.
fcn.takeaway: Class C = 254 usable host addresses (2^8 - 2)

fcn.q: for what purpose does the router "AND" the subnet mask to the destination address?
opts: to derive the host numbers so it knows where to sent the packet
to derive subnet mask and compare it with information in routing table
to derive which network and subnet to sent the packet to
fcn.expl: Class C has 8 host bits. Borrowing bits for subnets: up to 6 bits can be borrowed (keeping 2 for hosts).
fcn.takeaway: Class C subnets: borrow host bits (max 6 bits = 64 subnets)

fcn.q: with a class C of 197.15.22.31 and a subnet mask of 255.255.255.224, how many bits have been borrowed to created a subnet?
fcn.expl: Minimum bits to borrow for subnetting = 2 bits (gives 2^2=4 subnets, but only 2 usable with some conventions).
fcn.takeaway: Minimum subnet bits = 2 (for at least 2 usable subnets)

fcn.q: what is the minimum number of bits can be borrowed to form a subnet?
opts: none of the above 11 12 Session 1: Q.No.1. The maximum frame size supported by a communication link is known as? A: Load B: Reliability C: MTU D: Delay Session 2: Q.No.3. Which of the following IP Header field is used by the traceroute (tracert) command to find the route to the destination?
fcn.expl: Routers AND the IP address with the subnet mask to extract the network address, then compare to routing table entries.
fcn.takeaway: Router ANDs subnet mask to determine network address for routing

fcn.q: A 5. Parameter for zone
opts: 1-2,2-5,3-1,4-3,5-4 Q.No.13) Which of the following IP Header field changes from HOP to HOP during routing?
fcn.expl: This question tests knowledge of A 5. Parameter for zone... Review OSI model, TCP/IP stack, and networking fundamentals.
fcn.takeaway: Key networking concept: A 5. Parameter for zone

fcn.q: Which functions of the OSI model occur at layer two?
fcn.expl: Layer 2 (Data Link layer) handles framing, MAC addressing, error detection, and media access control.
fcn.takeaway: Layer 2 = framing, MAC addressing, error detection

fcn.q: A technician is asked to secure the privileged EXEC mode of a switch by requiring a password. Which type of password would require this login and be considered the most secure?
fcn.expl: The 'enable secret' command encrypts the privileged EXEC password using MD5, providing better security than 'enable password'.
fcn.takeaway: enable secret = encrypted privileged EXEC password (MD5)

fcn.q: Which OSI layer does IP rely on to determine whether packets have been lost and to request retransmission?
fcn.expl: IP relies on ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) to report errors and whether packets have arrived successfully.
fcn.takeaway: IP error reporting = ICMP (Layer 3 companion protocol)

fcn.q: When connectionless protocols are implemented at the lower layers of the OSI model, what is usually used to acknowledge that the data was received and to request the retransmission of missing data?
opts: an upper-layer, connection-oriented protocol or service
fcn.expl: When lower layers are connectionless, reliability must be implemented at higher layers (Transport/Application) if needed.
fcn.takeaway: Connectionless lower layers = reliability must be in upper layers

fcn.q: Which subnet mask will allow 2040 hosts per subnet on the IP network 10.0.0.0?
fcn.expl: 2040 hosts needs at least 11 bits (2^11=2048). Mask /21 = 255.255.248.0 provides 2046 hosts.
fcn.takeaway: 2040 hosts per subnet = /21 mask (255.255.248.0)

fcn.q: Which statement is true about the TTL value of an IPv4 packet?
opts: It determines the priority of a packet.
It specifies the remaining “life” of the packet.
It indicates that the packet has been fragmented.
It denotes the data payload type that the packet is carrying.
fcn.expl: TTL (Time to Live) is decremented by 1 at each router hop. When it reaches 0, the packet is discarded and ICMP Time Exceeded is sent.
fcn.takeaway: TTL: decremented by 1 at each router; packet dropped at 0

fcn.q: What type of network is maintained if a server takes no dedicated role in the network?
fcn.expl: A peer-to-peer (P2P) network has no dedicated servers; all computers can act as both client and server.
fcn.takeaway: No dedicated server = peer-to-peer (P2P) network

fcn.q: A routing issue has occurred in your internetwork. Which of the following type of devices should be examined to isolate this error?
fcn.expl: The traceroute command shows the path packets take and identifies where routing fails.
fcn.takeaway: Routing troubleshooting = traceroute (shows hop-by-hop path)

fcn.q: A user sees the command prompt: Router(config-if)#. What task can be performed at this mode?
fcn.expl: Router(config-if)# is interface configuration mode. The user is configuring a specific interface.
fcn.takeaway: Router(config-if)# = interface configuration mode

fcn.q: What information can be gathered by using the command netstat?
fcn.expl: The netstat command shows active connections, port states, routing tables, and network statistics.
fcn.takeaway: netstat = shows active connections, ports, routing table

fcn.q: How can ARP be used to help document the network?
opts: It can list each hop on the way to a destination host.
It can show the average latency of hosts on the network.
It can provide a list of physical addresses on the network.
It can indicate which hosts are not responding to network traffic.
fcn.expl: ARP cache/tables show IP-to-MAC mappings, helping document which devices are on each network segment.
fcn.takeaway: ARP = maps IP to MAC, helps document network devices

fcn.q: What is the protocol data unit that is associated with the data link layer of the OSI reference model?
fcn.expl: The Data Link layer PDU is called a Frame. Network layer=Packet, Transport=Segment, Physical=Bits.
fcn.takeaway: Data Link layer PDU = Frame

fcn.q: Which application layer protocol allows administrators to log into a server from remote locations and control a server as though logged in locally?
fcn.expl: Telnet is the application layer protocol for remote terminal access/login to network devices. SSH is more secure.
fcn.takeaway: Remote device login = Telnet (or SSH for secure)

fcn.q: Which statement identifies the DNS protocol?
opts: It transmits information using TCP port 25.
It transfers and conveys information using HTML pages.
It matches a resource name with the required network address.
It requests and assigns an IP address, default gateway, and server addresses to
fcn.expl: DNS (Domain Name System) resolves fully qualified domain names (FQDNs) to IP addresses.
fcn.takeaway: DNS = resolves domain names to IP addresses

fcn.q: Which password would allow a user to establish a Telnet session with a Cisco device?
fcn.expl: The VTY (virtual terminal) password controls Telnet/SSH access to network devices.
fcn.takeaway: Telnet access = VTY (virtual terminal) password

fcn.q: Which identifier is contained in the header of the Layer 2 Ethernet frame?
fcn.expl: Ethernet frames contain MAC (Media Access Control) addresses in the Layer 2 header.
fcn.takeaway: Layer 2 Ethernet header = MAC addresses

fcn.q: What happens when a node on an Ethernet network is creating a frame and it does not have the destination MAC address?
opts: The node sends out a Layer 3 broadcast message.
The node sends a message directly to the router for the address.
The node sends out an ARP request with the destination IP address.
fcn.expl: When creating a frame, the source node broadcasts an ARP request to find the MAC address for the destination IP.
fcn.takeaway: Frame creation = ARP broadcast to find destination MAC

fcn.q: An administrator makes changes to the default configuration of a router and saves them to NVRAM. After testing, it is discovered that the configurations did not have the desired effect and must be removed. What steps must be taken to remove the changes and return the router to the default configuration?
opts: Use no version of the commands that are issued.
Issue the erase startup-config command, and then reboot the router.
Issue the erase running-config command, and then reboot the router.
fcn.expl: Changes to running-config must be saved with 'copy running-config startup-config' to persist after reload.
fcn.takeaway: Save config = copy running-config startup-config

fcn.q: A network administrator is tasked with connecting two workgroups that are configured to use different subnets. Which device should be selected to allow connectivity between users on the two networks?
fcn.expl: A router connects different network segments/workgroups by routing traffic between them.
fcn.takeaway: Connecting different workgroups = Router (routes between networks)

fcn.q: Which program can be used to accomplish terminal emulation for configuring a router from the CLI through the console port?
fcn.expl: HyperTerminal (or similar terminal emulation software) is used to connect to routers/switches via console.
fcn.takeaway: Terminal emulation = HyperTerminal (console access)

fcn.q: Which statement accurately describes file sharing in a peer-to-peer network?
opts: Files are typically stored on the most powerful peer in the network.
Peers can either make requests, or respond to requests, but not both.
fcn.expl: In P2P networks, any node can share files and act as both server and client simultaneously.
fcn.takeaway: P2P file sharing = any node can share, no dedicated server

fcn.q: What is primary responsibilities of the Ethernet MAC sublayer?
fcn.expl: The MAC sublayer handles data encapsulation (framing) and media access control (CSMA/CD). Frame delimiting is part of framing.
fcn.takeaway: Ethernet MAC = data encapsulation + media access control

fcn.q: Which router configuration mode would an administrator use to configure the router for SSH or Telnet login access?
fcn.expl: Line configuration mode (line vty, line console) is used to configure Telnet/SSH access.
fcn.takeaway: SSH/Telnet config = line configuration mode

fcn.q: What purpose does a hostname serve on a router?
opts: uniquely identifies a router on the internet
used by routing protocols to identify peer routers
provides device identification to users logging on remotely
determines the hosts that are allowed to connect to the device
fcn.expl: A hostname identifies the device to users who log on remotely via Telnet/SSH.
fcn.takeaway: Hostname = identifies device to remote users

fcn.q: Which prompt is associated with the privileged exec mode?
fcn.expl: R1# indicates privileged EXEC mode (after 'enable' command). R1> is user EXEC mode.
fcn.takeaway: Privileged EXEC mode prompt = R1# (hash symbol)

fcn.q: A technician wishes to connect two computers to move data from one to the other before reloading the operating system. The NICs on both PCs do not support autosensing feature. Which procedure will satisfy the requirement?
opts: Interconnect the two PCs with a rollover cable.
Interconnect the two PCs with two rollover cables and a hub.
Interconnect the two PCs with a crossover cable.
Interconnect the two PCs with two crossover cables and a hub.
fcn.expl: A crossover cable is needed to directly connect two PCs without a switch, when NICs don't support auto-MDI/MDIX.
fcn.takeaway: Direct PC-to-PC connection = crossover cable

fcn.q: Two routers are connected via their serial ports in a lab environment. The routers are configured with valid IP addresses, but they cannot ping each other. The show interface serial0/0 command shows that the serial0/0 interface is up but the line protocol is down. What could be the cause of this problem?
opts: The no shutdown command has not been applied to the interfaces.
The clock rate command has not been entered on the DCE interface.
The interface timers are not cleared.
The Fast Ethernet interface is emulating a serial interface by assigning it a timing
fcn.expl: When serial interfaces are up but line protocol is down, the DCE side needs clock rate configured for timing.
fcn.takeaway: Serial link protocol down = clock rate not configured on DCE

fcn.q: An organization has been assigned network ID 10.10.128.0 and subnet mask 255.255.224.0. Which IP address range can be used for this organization?
When must a router serial interface be configured with the clock rate command?
opts: when the interface is functioning as a DTE device
when the interface timers have been cleared
when the connected DTE device is shut down
when the interface is functioning as a DCE device
fcn.expl: Clock rate is only configured on DCE (Data Circuit-terminating Equipment) interfaces to provide clocking signal.
fcn.takeaway: clock rate configured on = DCE interface only

fcn.q: Due to a security violation, the router passwords must be changed. What information can be learned from the following configuration entries? Router(config)# line vty 0 3 Router(config-line)# password c13c0 Router(config-line)# login
opts: The entries specify three Telnet lines for remote access.
The entries specify four Telnet lines for remote access.
The entries set the console and Telnet password to “c13c0″.
Telnet access will be denied because the Telnet configuration is incomplete.
fcn.expl: The config shows VTY lines 0-3 (lines 0 through 3 = 4 lines) with password c13c0 and login enabled.
fcn.takeaway: VTY 0 3 = 4 Telnet lines (0, 1, 2, 3)

fcn.q: Which password is automatically encrypted when it is created?
fcn.expl: 'enable secret' uses MD5 and is automatically encrypted. 'enable password' is plain text by default.
fcn.takeaway: Auto-encrypted password = enable secret (MD5 encrypted)

fcn.q: Which prompt represents the appropriate mode used for the copy running-config startup-config command?
fcn.expl: copy running-config startup-config requires privileged EXEC mode (# prompt).
fcn.takeaway: copy running-config = requires privileged EXEC mode (#)

fcn.q: Which information is used by the router to determine the path between the source and destination hosts?
opts: the host portion of the IP address
the network portion of the IP address
fcn.expl: Routers use the network portion of the destination IP address to look up routing table entries.
fcn.takeaway: Router routing decision = network portion of IP address

fcn.q: Well-known services and applications use which range of TCP/UDP port numbers?
fcn.expl: Well-known ports are 0-1023. Registered ports are 1024-49151. Dynamic/ephemeral ports are 49152-65535.
fcn.takeaway: Well-known ports = 0 to 1023

fcn.q: Which IPv4 address represents subnet broadcast addresses?
fcn.expl: For /26 subnets, each has 64 addresses. 192.168.4.63 is the broadcast of .0/26. 192.168.4.129 is host in .128/26.
fcn.takeaway: Subnet broadcast = last address in subnet range

fcn.q: Which statement is true about network layer addressing?
opts: Network layer addressing is used by Ethernet switches to make forwarding decisions.
A portion of the address is used to identify the network.
fcn.expl: TTL (Time to Live) is decremented by 1 at each router hop. When it reaches 0, the packet is discarded and ICMP Time Exceeded is sent.
fcn.takeaway: TTL: decremented by 1 at each router; packet dropped at 0

fcn.q: Which OSI layer addressing is required to route packets between two networks?
fcn.expl: Layer 3 (Network layer) addressing (IP addresses) is required for routing between different networks.
fcn.takeaway: Inter-network routing = Layer 3 (Network layer) addressing

fcn.q: How many host addresses may be assigned when using the 128.107.0.0 network address with a subnet mask of 255.255.248.0?
fcn.expl: Need to check specific subnet mask. /21 = 2046 hosts, typical for medium networks.
fcn.takeaway: Host count = 2^(host bits) - 2 (subtract network and broadcast)

fcn.q: During the encapsulation process, what is the PDU of the internet layer?
fcn.expl: The Internet layer (Network layer) PDU is called a packet. Transport=segment, Data Link=frame, Physical=bits.
fcn.takeaway: Internet layer PDU = packet

fcn.q: Which service is used when the nslookup command is issued on a computer?
fcn.expl: nslookup queries DNS (Domain Name System) to resolve hostnames to IP addresses.
fcn.takeaway: nslookup uses DNS service

fcn.q: What is the primary purpose of a communication system?
opts: To transmit information from a source to a destination
fcn.expl: A communication system's core purpose is to transfer information between a sender (source) and receiver (destination) over a medium.
fcn.takeaway: Communication system = transfers info from source to destination

fcn.q: Which of the following best describes 'bandwidth' in a communication system?
opts: The range of frequencies a medium can carry
fcn.expl: Bandwidth is the range of frequencies available for signal transmission. Higher bandwidth means more data can be transmitted per second.
fcn.takeaway: Bandwidth = frequency range; higher bandwidth = more data capacity

fcn.q: What is 'attenuation' in a communication system?
opts: Increase in signal strength over distance
Decrease in signal strength over distance
fcn.expl: Attenuation is the reduction of signal strength as it travels through a medium. It increases with distance and must be compensated by amplifiers or repeaters.
fcn.takeaway: Attenuation = signal strength loss over distance

fcn.q: Which type of transmission sends data in only one direction?
fcn.expl: Simplex communication is unidirectional — data flows only from sender to receiver. Example: radio broadcast, TV.
fcn.takeaway: Simplex = one direction only (e.g., TV broadcast)

fcn.q: In half-duplex communication, which of the following is true?
opts: Data flows in both directions simultaneously
Data flows in only one direction forever
Data flows in both directions but not simultaneously
fcn.expl: Half-duplex allows data transmission in both directions but only one direction at a time. Example: walkie-talkies.
fcn.takeaway: Half-duplex = both directions, but NOT simultaneously (walkie-talkie)

fcn.q: What is the Nyquist theorem used for?
opts: Determining maximum data rate for noiseless channels
fcn.expl: Nyquist theorem states: Max data rate = 2 x Bandwidth x log2(V), where V is the number of discrete signal levels. It applies to noiseless channels.
fcn.takeaway: Nyquist theorem = max data rate for noiseless channel (2 x BW x log2V)

fcn.q: The Shannon-Hartley theorem defines the theoretical maximum data rate considering:
opts: Only the bandwidth of the channel
fcn.expl: Shannon's theorem: Capacity = B x log2(1 + S/N), where B is bandwidth and S/N is signal-to-noise ratio. It gives the theoretical maximum for noisy channels.
fcn.takeaway: Shannon theorem = B x log2(1+SNR); considers both bandwidth AND noise

fcn.q: What does 'multiplexing' allow in communications?
opts: Multiple signals to share a single channel simultaneously
A single signal to split into multiple channels
fcn.expl: Multiplexing combines multiple signals onto a single transmission channel, improving efficiency. Types include FDM, TDM, WDM, and CDM.
fcn.takeaway: Multiplexing = multiple signals share ONE channel (FDM, TDM, WDM)

fcn.q: Which multiplexing technique divides bandwidth into frequency slots?
fcn.expl: FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing) assigns different frequency bands to each signal, allowing simultaneous transmission. Used in cable TV and radio.
fcn.takeaway: FDM = each signal gets its own frequency band

fcn.q: TDM stands for:
fcn.expl: TDM (Time Division Multiplexing) assigns each signal a time slot in a repeating cycle. Each source transmits during its designated slot.
fcn.takeaway: TDM = each signal gets its own time slot in a cycle

fcn.q: What is the difference between analog and digital signals?
opts: Analog is discrete, digital is continuous
Analog is continuous, digital is discrete
fcn.expl: Analog signals are continuous waveforms that vary smoothly. Digital signals use discrete values (0s and 1s). Most modern communication uses digital.
fcn.takeaway: Analog = continuous waveform; Digital = discrete values (0/1)

fcn.q: Which modulation technique changes the frequency of a carrier wave based on the data?
fcn.expl: FM (Frequency Modulation) varies the carrier frequency proportional to the input signal. It provides better noise immunity than AM.
fcn.takeaway: FM = carrier frequency varies with data signal

fcn.q: What is 'propagation delay' in networking?
opts: Time to process a packet at a router
Time for a bit to travel from sender to receiver
Time to retransmit a lost packet
fcn.expl: Propagation delay is the time it takes for a signal to travel from the source to the destination across the physical medium. It depends on distance and signal speed.
fcn.takeaway: Propagation delay = time for signal to travel from source to destination

fcn.q: Which layer of the OSI model handles modulation and demodulation?
fcn.expl: Modulation and demodulation occurs at the Physical layer (Layer 1), which deals with transmission of raw bits over physical media.
fcn.takeaway: Modulation/demodulation = Physical Layer (Layer 1)

fcn.q: What is 'noise' in a communication channel?
opts: Unwanted electrical signals that interfere with transmission
fcn.expl: Noise is any unwanted electrical energy that interferes with the desired signal, degrading signal quality and potentially causing bit errors.
fcn.takeaway: Noise = unwanted interference that degrades signal quality

fcn.q: The term 'baud rate' refers to:
fcn.expl: Baud rate is the number of symbol changes per second. Bit rate = Baud rate x bits per symbol. They are equal only when each symbol carries 1 bit.
fcn.takeaway: Baud rate = signal changes per second (symbols/sec, not bits/sec)

fcn.q: Which encoding scheme uses voltage transitions to represent data (transition = 1, no transition = 0)?
fcn.expl: NRZ-I (Non-Return to Zero Inverted) uses a voltage transition at the start of a 1-bit and no transition for a 0-bit. This helps with synchronization.
fcn.takeaway: NRZ-I = voltage transition at start = 1; no transition = 0

fcn.q: Manchester encoding is used in which standard?
fcn.expl: Manchester encoding is used in 10BASE-T Ethernet (original Ethernet). Each bit has a mid-bit transition: low to high = 1, high to low = 0. Provides self-clocking.
fcn.takeaway: Manchester encoding = 10BASE-T Ethernet (self-clocking mid-bit transitions)

fcn.q: What is the purpose of error detection in communication systems?
opts: To correct all transmission errors automatically
To identify that errors have occurred in transmission
fcn.expl: Error detection identifies when errors have occurred during transmission. Error correction (like FEC) actually fixes them. Common techniques: parity, CRC, checksums.
fcn.takeaway: Error detection = identifies errors occurred (CRC, parity, checksum)

fcn.q: CRC stands for:
fcn.expl: CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) is a powerful error detection method that uses polynomial division to generate a check value appended to the data.
fcn.takeaway: CRC = Cyclic Redundancy Check (strong error detection via polynomial division)

fcn.q: Which transmission medium uses light pulses to transmit data?
fcn.expl: Fiber optic cables transmit data as pulses of light through glass or plastic strands. They offer high bandwidth, low attenuation, and immunity to EMI.
fcn.takeaway: Fiber optic = light pulses; immune to EMI; highest bandwidth

fcn.q: Which type of fiber optic cable allows multiple light rays to travel at different angles?
fcn.expl: Multi-mode fiber has a larger core diameter allowing multiple light modes (paths) to travel simultaneously. Used for shorter distances (up to 550m for Gigabit).
fcn.takeaway: Multi-mode fiber = multiple light paths; shorter distances

fcn.q: Single-mode fiber optic cable is used for:
opts: Connecting switches in the same rack
fcn.expl: Single-mode fiber carries a single light ray along a narrow core, allowing very long distances (up to 100km) with minimal signal loss and dispersion.
fcn.takeaway: Single-mode fiber = long distances (100km+); one light path

fcn.q: What is the maximum segment length for Category 5e (Cat5e) UTP cable?
fcn.expl: Cat5e (and all Cat5/6/6a) UTP cables have a maximum segment length of 100 meters for 100BASE-TX and Gigabit Ethernet.
fcn.takeaway: Cat5e/Cat6 UTP max segment = 100 meters

fcn.q: Which cable type is most susceptible to electromagnetic interference (EMI)?
fcn.expl: UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair) has no shielding and is most susceptible to EMI. STP has a metallic shield, coaxial has ground shielding, and fiber is immune.
fcn.takeaway: UTP = most susceptible to EMI (no shielding)

fcn.q: What does STP stand for in cabling?
fcn.expl: STP (Shielded Twisted Pair) has a metallic foil or braid shielding around the wire pairs to reduce electromagnetic interference. More expensive than UTP.
fcn.takeaway: STP (cabling) = Shielded Twisted Pair (reduces EMI with metallic shield)

fcn.q: Which wireless frequency band offers higher speed but shorter range?
fcn.expl: The 5 GHz band offers higher data rates (less congestion, more channels) but shorter range due to higher frequency absorption. 2.4 GHz has better range.
fcn.takeaway: 5 GHz = higher speed, shorter range; 2.4 GHz = longer range, more congestion

fcn.q: Which 802.11 standard operates ONLY in the 5 GHz band?
fcn.expl: 802.11a operates exclusively in the 5 GHz band at up to 54 Mbps. 802.11b/g operate at 2.4 GHz. 802.11n operates on both bands.
fcn.takeaway: 802.11a = 5 GHz only, up to 54 Mbps

fcn.q: The maximum theoretical throughput of 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) can reach:
fcn.expl: 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) operates in the 5 GHz band with theoretical throughputs exceeding 1 Gbps using MU-MIMO and wide channels (up to 160 MHz).
fcn.takeaway: 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) = 5 GHz, 1 Gbps+ (MU-MIMO)

fcn.q: What is the primary advantage of coaxial cable over UTP?
opts: Better noise immunity and higher bandwidth
fcn.expl: Coaxial cable provides better shielding (outer conductor shields inner conductor) and can carry signals over longer distances with better noise resistance than UTP.
fcn.takeaway: Coaxial advantage = better noise immunity and bandwidth vs UTP

fcn.q: In satellite communication, what causes the significant delay?
opts: Propagation delay due to long distance
fcn.expl: Geostationary satellites orbit at ~36,000 km. The round-trip propagation delay is approximately 480-600ms, making real-time applications like voice calls difficult.
fcn.takeaway: Satellite delay = propagation delay from 36,000km orbit (~500ms RTT)

fcn.q: Which transmission medium is completely immune to electromagnetic interference?
fcn.expl: Fiber optic cable transmits light, not electrical signals, making it completely immune to EMI/RFI. It also cannot be tapped without detection.
fcn.takeaway: Fiber optic = completely immune to EMI/RFI (uses light, not electricity)

fcn.q: What is the purpose of the RJ-45 connector?
opts: To connect UTP/STP twisted pair cables
fcn.expl: RJ-45 is an 8-position, 8-contact modular connector used for UTP/STP Ethernet cabling. It is the standard connector for Cat5e/Cat6 Ethernet.
fcn.takeaway: RJ-45 = 8-pin connector for UTP/STP Ethernet cables

fcn.q: A crossover cable is used to connect:
fcn.expl: Crossover cables swap TX and RX pairs, allowing direct connection between similar devices (PC-to-PC, switch-to-switch). Modern devices with auto-MDI/MDIX can use straight-through cables.
fcn.takeaway: Crossover cable = same device type to same device type (PC-PC, switch-switch)

fcn.q: What cable standard defines T568A and T568B pinouts?
fcn.expl: TIA/EIA-568 defines T568A and T568B wiring standards for UTP Ethernet. T568B is more common in North America.
fcn.takeaway: TIA/EIA-568 = defines T568A/T568B pinout standards for UTP

fcn.q: What is 'crosstalk' in cabling?
fcn.expl: Crosstalk is unwanted signal coupling between adjacent wire pairs. Twisting wire pairs reduces crosstalk by canceling out induced signals.
fcn.takeaway: Crosstalk = interference between adjacent wire pairs (twisting reduces it)

fcn.q: Microwave communication requires:
fcn.expl: Microwave signals travel in a straight line and require unobstructed line-of-sight between antennas. Physical obstacles (buildings, terrain) block the signal.
fcn.takeaway: Microwave = requires line-of-sight (LOS) between antennas

fcn.q: Which transmission medium is used for long-distance undersea communications?
fcn.expl: Submarine fiber optic cables carry the vast majority of international internet and telecommunications traffic under the oceans. They offer very high capacity and low latency.
fcn.takeaway: Undersea long-distance communication = submarine fiber optic cables

fcn.q: What does 'full duplex' mean in the context of Ethernet?
opts: Data travels in one direction only
Data travels in both directions simultaneously using separate channels
Data travels in both directions but not simultaneously
Data is broadcast to all devices
fcn.expl: Full-duplex Ethernet uses separate transmit and receive pairs (or frequencies), allowing simultaneous bidirectional data flow. This doubles throughput vs half-duplex.
fcn.takeaway: Full-duplex = simultaneous bidirectional communication (separate TX/RX)

fcn.q: What is the maximum distance for a point-to-point microwave link?
fcn.expl: Terrestrial microwave links are limited to about 50 km due to Earth's curvature and line-of-sight requirements. Towers are placed at regular intervals for longer distances.
fcn.takeaway: Terrestrial microwave max range = ~50 km (Earth curvature limits LOS)

fcn.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
fcn.expl: The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model has 7 layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application.
fcn.takeaway: OSI model = 7 layers (Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away)

fcn.q: Which OSI layer is responsible for end-to-end error recovery and flow control?
fcn.expl: The Transport Layer (Layer 4) provides end-to-end communication, error recovery, flow control, and segmentation. TCP and UDP operate here.
fcn.takeaway: Transport Layer (L4) = end-to-end error recovery + flow control (TCP/UDP)

fcn.q: Which OSI layer handles logical addressing and routing?
fcn.expl: The Network Layer (Layer 3) handles logical addressing (IP addresses) and routing - determining the best path for packets through internetworks.
fcn.takeaway: Network Layer (L3) = logical addressing (IP) + routing

fcn.q: The Session Layer of the OSI model is responsible for:
opts: Establishing, managing, and terminating communication sessions
fcn.expl: The Session Layer (Layer 5) establishes, manages, and terminates communication sessions between applications. It handles dialog control and synchronization.
fcn.takeaway: Session Layer (L5) = establishes, manages, terminates sessions

fcn.q: Which layer converts data into a format suitable for transmission (encryption, compression)?
fcn.expl: The Presentation Layer (Layer 6) handles data translation, encryption, and compression. It ensures data is in a format both communicating systems can understand.
