fcn.takeaway: Presentation Layer (L6) = encryption, compression, data translation

fcn.q: Which OSI layer provides the user interface and application services like HTTP, FTP, SMTP?
fcn.expl: The Application Layer (Layer 7) is the topmost layer and provides network services directly to end-user applications. Protocols include HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS.
fcn.takeaway: Application Layer (L7) = user-facing services (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS)

fcn.q: The Data Link layer is divided into which two sublayers?
fcn.expl: The Data Link layer (Layer 2) consists of the MAC (Media Access Control) sublayer - controlling media access - and the LLC (Logical Link Control) sublayer.
fcn.takeaway: Data Link Layer = MAC sublayer + LLC sublayer

fcn.q: Which device operates at the Physical layer (Layer 1) of the OSI model?
fcn.expl: Hubs and repeaters operate at Layer 1 (Physical). They simply regenerate electrical signals without any intelligence. Bridges/switches work at Layer 2, routers at Layer 3.
fcn.takeaway: Layer 1 devices = Hub, Repeater (signal regeneration only)

fcn.q: Which device operates at Layer 2 (Data Link) of the OSI model?
fcn.expl: Switches and bridges operate at Layer 2. They use MAC addresses to make forwarding decisions. Switches have replaced hubs in modern networks.
fcn.takeaway: Layer 2 devices = Switch, Bridge (uses MAC addresses)

fcn.q: What is the PDU (Protocol Data Unit) at the Network layer?
fcn.expl: The PDU at Layer 3 (Network) is called a Packet. Layer 2 = Frame, Layer 4 = Segment, Layer 1 = Bit.
fcn.takeaway: PDUs: L1=Bit, L2=Frame, L3=Packet, L4=Segment

fcn.q: The process of adding headers/trailers at each layer as data travels down the OSI stack is called:
fcn.expl: Encapsulation is the process of wrapping data with protocol headers (and trailers) at each layer as it travels down the OSI stack from Application to Physical.
fcn.takeaway: Encapsulation = adding headers/trailers going DOWN the OSI stack

fcn.q: At which OSI layer does SSL/TLS operate?
fcn.expl: SSL/TLS operates between the Transport and Application layers, spanning the Session and Presentation layers in the OSI model. It provides encryption and authentication.
fcn.takeaway: SSL/TLS = operates at Session/Presentation layer (L5/L6)

fcn.q: Which OSI layer provides flow control using a sliding window mechanism?
fcn.expl: The Transport layer (Layer 4) provides flow control using windowing (sliding window), which controls how much data can be sent before waiting for acknowledgment.
fcn.takeaway: Windowing/flow control = Transport Layer (L4) - TCP sliding window

fcn.q: Which IEEE standard defines the Data Link layer for Ethernet?
fcn.expl: IEEE 802.3 defines the Ethernet standard, including both the Physical layer and the Data Link layer (MAC sublayer) for wired Ethernet networks.
fcn.takeaway: IEEE 802.3 = Ethernet (defines both Physical and Data Link MAC sublayer)

fcn.q: What is the purpose of the trailer added by the Data Link layer?
fcn.expl: The Data Link layer adds a trailer containing the FCS (Frame Check Sequence) - a CRC value used to detect transmission errors in the frame.
fcn.takeaway: Data Link trailer = FCS/CRC for error detection

fcn.q: Which protocol maps IP addresses to MAC addresses at Layer 2?
fcn.expl: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) operates between Layer 2 and Layer 3 to resolve a known IP address to the corresponding MAC address for local delivery.
fcn.takeaway: ARP = maps IP address to MAC address (Layer 3 to Layer 2)

fcn.q: What is the OSI layer responsible for establishing a TCP connection (3-way handshake)?
fcn.expl: The TCP 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) occurs at the Transport layer (Layer 4). TCP is a Transport layer protocol.
fcn.takeaway: TCP 3-way handshake = Transport Layer (L4)

fcn.q: In the OSI model, which layer is responsible for converting bits to electrical signals?
fcn.expl: The Physical layer (Layer 1) converts digital bits into electrical, optical, or radio signals for transmission over the physical medium.
fcn.takeaway: Bits to electrical/optical signals = Physical Layer (L1)

fcn.q: Which OSI layer uses ports to identify applications?
fcn.expl: The Transport layer uses port numbers to identify specific applications or services. TCP/UDP port numbers direct data to the correct application on the host.
fcn.takeaway: Port numbers = Transport Layer (L4) - identifies applications

fcn.q: The concept of 'peer-to-peer communication' in OSI means:
opts: Direct physical connection between two devices
Each layer on sender communicates logically with the same layer on receiver
Two devices sharing files without a server
fcn.expl: In OSI, each layer communicates logically with its peer layer on the remote system using protocol headers. Actual data passes down/up through all layers.
fcn.takeaway: OSI peer communication = same layer on sender talks to same layer on receiver

fcn.q: Which OSI layer is responsible for name resolution (DNS)?
fcn.expl: DNS operates at the Application layer (Layer 7). It resolves human-readable domain names to IP addresses used by the Network layer.
fcn.takeaway: DNS = Application Layer (L7)

fcn.q: A router operates at which OSI layer?
fcn.expl: Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network). They make forwarding decisions based on IP addresses and routing tables to route packets between different networks.
fcn.takeaway: Router = Layer 3 (Network) - routes using IP addresses

fcn.q: What does the Physical layer define?
opts: Cables, connectors, voltages, and signal encoding
fcn.expl: The Physical layer specifies hardware standards: cable types, connector specifications, electrical voltages, signal frequencies, and data encoding methods.
fcn.takeaway: Physical Layer = cables, connectors, voltages, signal encoding

fcn.q: Which OSI layer handles dialog control (who transmits, when)?
fcn.expl: The Session Layer (Layer 5) handles dialog control - managing whether communication is simplex, half-duplex, or full-duplex, and who transmits when.
fcn.takeaway: Dialog control = Session Layer (L5)

fcn.q: The LLC sublayer of the Data Link layer is defined by which standard?
fcn.expl: IEEE 802.2 defines the LLC (Logical Link Control) sublayer, which provides an interface between the Network layer and the MAC sublayer, regardless of the physical medium.
fcn.takeaway: LLC sublayer = IEEE 802.2 (independent of physical medium)

fcn.q: How many layers does the TCP/IP model have?
fcn.expl: The TCP/IP model has 4 layers: Network Access (Link), Internet, Transport, and Application. It maps to the 7-layer OSI model.
fcn.takeaway: TCP/IP model = 4 layers (Network Access, Internet, Transport, Application)

fcn.q: Which TCP/IP layer corresponds to both the OSI Physical and Data Link layers?
fcn.expl: The TCP/IP Network Access layer (also called Link layer) encompasses both the OSI Physical layer and Data Link layer functions.
fcn.takeaway: TCP/IP Network Access = OSI Physical + Data Link (Layers 1+2)

fcn.q: Which TCP/IP layer corresponds to the OSI Network layer?
fcn.expl: The TCP/IP Internet layer corresponds to OSI Layer 3 (Network). It handles IP addressing, routing, and packet forwarding using IP, ICMP, ARP.
fcn.takeaway: TCP/IP Internet layer = OSI Network Layer (IP, ICMP operate here)

fcn.q: Which protocol in the TCP/IP suite provides reliable, connection-oriented transport?
fcn.expl: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) provides reliable, connection-oriented, ordered, and error-checked delivery of data at the Transport layer.
fcn.takeaway: TCP = reliable, connection-oriented, ordered delivery (Transport layer)

fcn.q: Which protocol in the TCP/IP suite provides fast, connectionless transport?
fcn.expl: UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is connectionless and provides fast, best-effort delivery without reliability mechanisms. Used for streaming, DNS, VoIP.
fcn.takeaway: UDP = fast, connectionless, best-effort (DNS, VoIP, streaming)

fcn.q: Which well-known port does HTTP use?
fcn.expl: HTTP uses TCP port 80. HTTPS uses port 443. FTP uses port 21. Telnet uses port 23.
fcn.takeaway: HTTP = TCP port 80; HTTPS = port 443

fcn.q: Which port does FTP use for data transfer?
fcn.expl: FTP uses two ports: port 21 for control (commands) and port 20 for data transfer. SSH uses port 22, Telnet uses port 23.
fcn.takeaway: FTP: port 21 = control, port 20 = data transfer

fcn.q: Which port does SSH use?
fcn.expl: SSH (Secure Shell) uses TCP port 22. It provides encrypted remote access, replacing insecure Telnet (port 23).
fcn.takeaway: SSH = TCP port 22 (encrypted replacement for Telnet port 23)

fcn.q: Which port does DNS use?
fcn.expl: DNS uses port 53 (both TCP and UDP). TCP for zone transfers, UDP for normal queries. DHCP uses ports 67/68, TFTP uses port 69.
fcn.takeaway: DNS = port 53 (UDP for queries, TCP for zone transfers)

fcn.q: DHCP uses which ports?
fcn.expl: DHCP server listens on UDP port 67, clients use UDP port 68. The discover/offer/request/acknowledgment messages use these ports.
fcn.takeaway: DHCP = Server port 67, Client port 68 (UDP)

fcn.q: Which protocol uses port 25?
fcn.expl: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) uses TCP port 25 for sending email. POP3 uses port 110, IMAP uses port 143.
fcn.takeaway: SMTP = TCP port 25 (sending email)

fcn.q: What does ICMP stand for?
fcn.expl: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) is used for error reporting and diagnostic functions. Tools like ping and traceroute use ICMP.
fcn.takeaway: ICMP = Internet Control Message Protocol (ping, traceroute)

fcn.q: The ping command uses which ICMP message types?
fcn.expl: Ping sends ICMP Echo Request messages and expects ICMP Echo Reply responses. If no reply, it indicates the host is unreachable or blocking ICMP.
fcn.takeaway: Ping = ICMP Echo Request (type 8) + Echo Reply (type 0)

fcn.q: Traceroute works by sending packets with incrementing:
fcn.expl: Traceroute sends packets with TTL starting at 1, increasing by 1 each hop. Each router decrements TTL; when TTL=0, it sends ICMP Time Exceeded back, revealing the router's IP.
fcn.takeaway: Traceroute = increments TTL each probe; routers return ICMP Time Exceeded

fcn.q: Which TCP/IP Application layer protocol is used for transferring files?
fcn.expl: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is used to transfer files between a client and server. It uses TCP ports 20 (data) and 21 (control).
fcn.takeaway: FTP = File Transfer Protocol (TCP ports 20 data, 21 control)

fcn.q: SNMP is used for:
fcn.expl: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) is used to monitor and manage network devices (routers, switches, servers). It uses UDP port 161 (queries) and 162 (traps).
fcn.takeaway: SNMP = network management/monitoring (UDP 161 queries, 162 traps)

fcn.q: Which TCP mechanism prevents a receiver's buffer from being overwhelmed?
fcn.expl: TCP flow control uses a sliding window mechanism where the receiver advertises its buffer size (receive window). The sender cannot exceed this window size.
fcn.takeaway: TCP flow control = sliding window prevents receiver buffer overflow

fcn.q: What is the TCP 3-way handshake sequence?
fcn.expl: TCP connection establishment: Client sends SYN -> Server responds SYN-ACK -> Client sends ACK. After this, the connection is established and data transfer begins.
fcn.takeaway: TCP handshake = SYN -> SYN-ACK -> ACK (client initiates)

fcn.q: What is the maximum size of a TCP segment header (without options)?
fcn.expl: The minimum TCP header size is 20 bytes (without options). With options (like timestamps), it can be up to 60 bytes. The UDP header is always 8 bytes.
fcn.takeaway: TCP header minimum = 20 bytes; UDP header = always 8 bytes

fcn.q: Which TCP/IP protocol is responsible for logical-to-physical address resolution?
fcn.expl: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) is part of the TCP/IP Internet layer. It resolves known IP addresses to MAC addresses for local network delivery.
fcn.takeaway: ARP = TCP/IP Internet layer; resolves IP to MAC

fcn.q: What does IOS stand for in Cisco terminology?
fcn.expl: Cisco IOS stands for Internetwork Operating System - the software that runs on Cisco routers and switches, providing routing, switching, and security features.
fcn.takeaway: Cisco IOS = Internetwork Operating System

fcn.q: Which Cisco IOS mode allows viewing router status but NOT making configuration changes?
fcn.expl: User EXEC mode (prompt: Router>) allows only viewing basic information. Privileged EXEC (Router#) allows more commands. Global Config (Router(config)#) allows changes.
fcn.takeaway: User EXEC mode (Router>) = view only, no configuration changes

fcn.q: How do you enter Privileged EXEC mode from User EXEC mode?
fcn.expl: The 'enable' command (or 'en') transitions from User EXEC mode (Router>) to Privileged EXEC mode (Router#), where more powerful commands are available.
fcn.takeaway: Router> to Router# = type 'enable' command

fcn.q: Which command enters Global Configuration mode from Privileged EXEC?
fcn.expl: 'configure terminal' (or 'config t') from Privileged EXEC mode (Router#) enters Global Configuration mode (Router(config)#).
fcn.takeaway: Router# to Router(config)# = 'configure terminal' or 'config t'

fcn.q: Which command shows the running configuration of a Cisco router?
fcn.expl: 'show running-config' displays the current active configuration in RAM. 'show startup-config' shows the saved configuration in NVRAM.
fcn.takeaway: show running-config = current active config (RAM); show startup-config = saved (NVRAM)

fcn.q: Where is the Cisco router running configuration stored?
fcn.expl: The running configuration is stored in RAM and is lost when the router is powered off. The startup configuration is saved in NVRAM and persists across reboots.
fcn.takeaway: Running config = RAM (volatile); Startup config = NVRAM (persistent)

fcn.q: Which command saves the running configuration to NVRAM on a Cisco router?
fcn.expl: 'copy running-config startup-config' (or 'write memory' / 'wr') saves the active RAM configuration to NVRAM so it persists after reboot.
fcn.takeaway: Save config = 'copy running-config startup-config' (or 'wr')

fcn.q: The Cisco IOS 'show ip interface brief' command displays:
opts: Summary of interface IP addresses and status
fcn.expl: 'show ip interface brief' shows a summary of all interfaces: IP address, subnet mask (abbreviated), and status (up/up, up/down, administratively down/down).
fcn.takeaway: show ip interface brief = summary of all interfaces IP + status

fcn.q: What does 'no shutdown' do on a Cisco router interface?
fcn.expl: By default, router interfaces are 'administratively down'. The 'no shutdown' command enables (activates) the interface, changing its state from admin down to up.
fcn.takeaway: no shutdown = enables/activates an interface (removes admin down)

fcn.q: Which command assigns an IP address to a Cisco router interface?
fcn.expl: Under interface configuration mode: 'ip address [IP_address] [subnet_mask]' assigns the IP address. Then 'no shutdown' to activate.
fcn.takeaway: Assign IP to interface = 'ip address [IP] [mask]' in interface config mode

fcn.q: Cisco SDM stands for:
fcn.expl: Cisco SDM (Security Device Manager) is a web-based GUI tool for configuring Cisco routers. It was replaced by Cisco Configuration Professional (CCP).
fcn.takeaway: Cisco SDM = Security Device Manager (web-based GUI for router config)

fcn.q: Which Cisco IOS command displays the routing table?
fcn.expl: 'show ip route' displays the routing table, showing all known networks, the routing protocol that learned them, next-hop addresses, and exit interfaces.
fcn.takeaway: show ip route = displays the complete routing table

fcn.q: In Cisco IOS, what does the 'C' prefix in 'show ip route' output indicate?
fcn.expl: In 'show ip route', C = directly Connected network, S = Static, R = RIP, O = OSPF, D = EIGRP, * = default route.
fcn.takeaway: Route codes: C=Connected, S=Static, R=RIP, O=OSPF, D=EIGRP

fcn.q: What is the purpose of the 'enable secret' command in Cisco IOS?
opts: Sets a plain-text privileged EXEC password
Sets an MD5-encrypted privileged EXEC password
fcn.expl: 'enable secret [password]' sets a strong MD5-hashed password for Privileged EXEC mode. It supersedes 'enable password' and is always encrypted.
fcn.takeaway: enable secret = MD5-encrypted privileged EXEC password (always encrypted)

fcn.q: Which command enables password encryption for all plain-text passwords in IOS config?
fcn.expl: 'service password-encryption' applies Cisco Type 7 (weak) encryption to all plain-text passwords in the config. 'enable secret' uses stronger MD5 (Type 5).
fcn.takeaway: service password-encryption = Type 7 encryption for all plain-text passwords

fcn.q: What is the Cisco IOS 'banner motd' command used for?
opts: Displaying a message of the day to users who connect
fcn.expl: 'banner motd [delimiter] message [delimiter]' configures a Message of the Day (MOTD) banner displayed to users when they connect to the device.
fcn.takeaway: banner motd = Message of the Day displayed to users upon connection

fcn.q: Which Cisco IOS command configures a description on an interface?
fcn.expl: Under interface configuration mode, 'description [text]' adds a description to help identify the interface's purpose in show commands and documentation.
fcn.takeaway: description [text] = adds documentation comment to interface

fcn.q: What does 'show version' display on a Cisco router?
opts: IOS version, hardware info, uptime, and license
fcn.expl: 'show version' displays the IOS version, hardware platform, RAM/Flash, interfaces, configuration register, and system uptime.
fcn.takeaway: show version = IOS version, hardware info, uptime, config register

fcn.q: The 'reload' command in Cisco IOS:
opts: Reloads the running config from NVRAM
fcn.expl: 'reload' causes the router to reboot. If changes have not been saved with 'copy run start', they are lost. The startup-config is loaded from NVRAM on boot.
fcn.takeaway: reload = reboots the router (unsaved changes are lost)

fcn.q: Which IOS command removes an IP address from an interface?
fcn.expl: The 'no' prefix reverses any IOS command. 'no ip address' removes the configured IP address from the interface.
fcn.takeaway: 'no' prefix removes configuration; 'no ip address' removes interface IP

fcn.q: What is the Cisco IOS command to set the router hostname?
fcn.expl: 'hostname [name]' in Global Configuration mode changes the router's name, which appears in the command prompt and is used for identification.
fcn.takeaway: hostname [name] = sets router name (in global config mode)

fcn.q: Which command backs up a Cisco IOS configuration to a TFTP server?
fcn.expl: 'copy running-config tftp' copies the running configuration to a TFTP server for backup. You will be prompted for the TFTP server IP and filename.
fcn.takeaway: copy running-config tftp = backup config to TFTP server

fcn.q: The 'show interfaces' command in Cisco IOS provides:
opts: Detailed interface statistics including errors, packets, and encapsulation
fcn.expl: 'show interfaces' shows detailed statistics for each interface: status, bandwidth, delays, input/output errors, packet counts, and encapsulation type.
fcn.takeaway: show interfaces = detailed statistics: errors, packets, encapsulation

fcn.q: What does 'show cdp neighbors' display?
opts: Connected Cisco devices and their details
fcn.expl: CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) discovers directly connected Cisco devices. 'show cdp neighbors' lists connected Cisco devices, their interfaces, and device type.
fcn.takeaway: show cdp neighbors = lists directly connected Cisco devices

fcn.q: Which command clears the ARP cache on a Cisco router?
fcn.expl: 'clear ip arp' clears all entries from the ARP cache on the router, forcing it to re-learn MAC-to-IP mappings through ARP requests.
fcn.takeaway: clear ip arp = clears ARP cache on router

fcn.q: What is the purpose of loopback interfaces on a Cisco router?
opts: To test the router itself (always up, virtual interface)
fcn.expl: Loopback interfaces are virtual and always in 'up/up' state. Used for testing, management, and as router-IDs for OSPF/BGP. They do not require physical hardware.
fcn.takeaway: Loopback interface = always up virtual interface; used for management, OSPF router-ID

fcn.q: How do you configure a clock rate on a serial DCE interface in Cisco IOS?
fcn.expl: On DCE serial interfaces, 'clock rate [bps]' sets the clocking signal. DTE interfaces receive the clock. DCE side is determined by 'show controllers' output.
fcn.takeaway: clock rate [speed] = configured on DCE serial interface to provide clocking

fcn.q: Which command shows the serial interface type (DCE or DTE)?
fcn.expl: 'show controllers serial [interface]' shows whether the interface is DCE or DTE based on the cable type connected. DCE must configure 'clock rate'.
fcn.takeaway: show controllers serial = shows DCE or DTE cable type

fcn.q: What does 'debug ip routing' display on a Cisco router?
fcn.expl: 'debug ip routing' shows real-time changes to the routing table - when routes are added, removed, or modified. Use with caution on production routers.
fcn.takeaway: debug ip routing = real-time routing table changes (use with caution)

fcn.q: What is the purpose of a console password in Cisco IOS?
opts: Secures the physical console port access
fcn.expl: A console password protects the physical console port (direct connection). Set under 'line console 0' with 'password [pw]' and 'login' commands.
fcn.takeaway: Console password = secures physical console port (line console 0)

fcn.q: Which IOS command sets the VTY password for Telnet access?
fcn.expl: To set Telnet/SSH passwords: 'line vty 0 4' (enters VTY line config), 'password [pw]', 'login'. This allows up to 5 simultaneous Telnet sessions.
fcn.takeaway: VTY password = line vty 0 4 -> password [pw] -> login

fcn.q: What does the IOS command 'ip default-gateway' configure on a switch?
fcn.expl: 'ip default-gateway [IP]' on a Layer 2 switch sets the gateway used for management traffic. It allows the switch itself to send management packets off-subnet.
fcn.takeaway: ip default-gateway = management default gateway on Layer 2 switch

fcn.q: The Cisco IOS 'show flash' command displays:
fcn.expl: 'show flash' shows the contents of flash memory, including the IOS image file name, size, and available free space in flash.
fcn.takeaway: show flash = flash memory contents (IOS image filename and size)

fcn.q: Which command erases the startup configuration on a Cisco router?
fcn.expl: 'erase startup-config' (or 'write erase') removes the startup configuration from NVRAM. After reload, the router will start in initial setup mode.
fcn.takeaway: erase startup-config = removes config from NVRAM (factory reset)

fcn.q: What is the default administrative distance of a static route in Cisco IOS?
fcn.expl: Static routes have an administrative distance (AD) of 1. Connected routes = 0, EIGRP = 90, OSPF = 110, RIP = 120. Lower AD = more preferred.
fcn.takeaway: Static route AD = 1; Connected = 0; RIP = 120; OSPF = 110; EIGRP = 90

fcn.q: What is a LAN?
opts: Network confined to a limited geographic area (building/campus)
Network that spans an entire country
fcn.expl: A LAN (Local Area Network) is a network that covers a limited geographic area such as a single building, floor, or campus. It typically uses Ethernet or Wi-Fi.
fcn.takeaway: LAN = Local Area Network (limited area: building/campus)

fcn.q: What is the CSMA/CD access method used in Ethernet?
fcn.expl: CSMA/CD: Carrier Sense (listen before transmit), Multiple Access (multiple devices share medium), Collision Detection (detect and handle collisions by backing off randomly).
fcn.takeaway: CSMA/CD = Carrier Sense Multiple Access/Collision Detection (shared Ethernet)

fcn.q: In modern switched Ethernet, why is CSMA/CD rarely used?
opts: Each switch port is a dedicated collision domain (full-duplex eliminates collisions)
Modern Ethernet uses optical fiber which prevents collisions
fcn.expl: Modern switches create a separate collision domain per port. With full-duplex operation, devices can send and receive simultaneously, making collisions impossible.
fcn.takeaway: Switched Ethernet + full-duplex = no collisions (CSMA/CD not needed)

fcn.q: What is the size range of an Ethernet frame (including header and FCS)?
fcn.expl: Ethernet frames must be between 64 bytes (minimum, to detect collisions) and 1518 bytes (maximum, standard MTU). Frames smaller than 64 bytes are 'runts'.
fcn.takeaway: Ethernet frame size = 64 bytes (min) to 1518 bytes (max)

fcn.q: What is a 'broadcast domain'?
opts: Area where all devices share the same collision domain
Area where broadcast frames are received by all devices
Area covered by a single switch
Area covered by a single VLAN only
fcn.expl: A broadcast domain is the set of all devices that receive a Layer 2 broadcast frame. Routers separate broadcast domains. VLANs create separate broadcast domains within a switch.
fcn.takeaway: Broadcast domain = all devices that receive L2 broadcast (routers separate them)

fcn.q: What is a 'collision domain'?
opts: Area where all broadcast traffic is received
Network segment where two devices transmitting simultaneously cause a collision
Area managed by a single switch
fcn.expl: A collision domain is a network segment where simultaneous transmissions from two devices can collide. Switches separate collision domains per port; hubs share one collision domain.
fcn.takeaway: Collision domain = segment where simultaneous TX can collide (switch = separate per port)

fcn.q: How many collision domains does a 24-port switch create?
fcn.expl: Each port on a switch is a separate collision domain. A 24-port switch creates 24 collision domains (one per port). But all ports are in the same broadcast domain (unless VLANs).
fcn.takeaway: 24-port switch = 24 collision domains (one per port)

fcn.q: What is the purpose of a MAC address table on a switch?
opts: To map MAC addresses to switch ports for forwarding decisions
To store IP addresses of connected devices
fcn.expl: The MAC address table (CAM table) maps source MAC addresses to the port they were learned on. This allows the switch to unicast frames to the correct port instead of flooding.
fcn.takeaway: MAC address table = maps MAC addresses to ports (reduces flooding)

fcn.q: When a switch receives a frame with an unknown destination MAC, it:
opts: Sends the frame back to source
Floods the frame out all ports except the source port
fcn.expl: When a switch does not know which port a destination MAC is on, it floods the frame out all ports except the receiving port. This is called 'unknown unicast flooding'.
fcn.takeaway: Unknown destination MAC = switch floods frame to ALL ports except source

fcn.q: What is EtherChannel (Link Aggregation)?
opts: A method to extend Ethernet over WAN
Combining multiple physical links into one logical link for increased bandwidth
fcn.expl: EtherChannel bundles multiple physical Ethernet links into a single logical link, increasing bandwidth and providing redundancy. IEEE 802.3ad (LACP) is the standard.
fcn.takeaway: EtherChannel = bundles multiple links into one logical link (more bandwidth + redundancy)

fcn.q: Which Gigabit Ethernet standard uses four pairs of Cat5e/Cat6 UTP?
fcn.expl: 1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet over copper) uses all four pairs of Cat5e or Cat6 UTP cable over a maximum distance of 100 meters.
fcn.takeaway: 1000BASE-T = Gigabit over Cat5e/Cat6 UTP (4 pairs, 100m max)

fcn.q: What does CSMA/CA stand for and where is it used?
opts: Carrier Sense Multiple Access/Collision Avoidance - used in Wi-Fi
Carrier Sense Multiple Access/Collision Acknowledgment - used in Ethernet
Carrier Sharing Multiple Access/Collision Acceptance - used in Token Ring
Channel Synchronization/Multiple Access/Collision Acknowledgment - used in WAN
fcn.expl: CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access/Collision Avoidance) is used in Wi-Fi (802.11). Since wireless networks cannot detect collisions, they try to AVOID them.
fcn.takeaway: CSMA/CA = Wi-Fi (802.11); CSMA/CD = Wired Ethernet (legacy)

fcn.q: What is a 'trunk link' on a switch?
opts: A link that carries traffic for only one VLAN
A high-speed link between switches that carries traffic for multiple VLANs
A link reserved for management traffic
A link that connects a switch to a router
fcn.expl: A trunk link carries traffic for multiple VLANs by using VLAN tagging (802.1Q). It is typically used between switches or between a switch and a router.
fcn.takeaway: Trunk link = carries multiple VLANs using 802.1Q tagging (switch-to-switch)

fcn.q: What is the MAC flooding attack?
opts: Filling the switch CAM table with fake MACs so switch floods like a hub enabling sniffing
fcn.expl: A MAC flooding attack fills the switch's CAM table with fake MAC addresses. When the table is full, the switch starts flooding all frames like a hub, allowing sniffing.
fcn.takeaway: MAC flooding attack = fills CAM table to switch floods like hub (sniffing possible)

fcn.q: What is the purpose of port security on a Cisco switch?
opts: To encrypt traffic on a port
To limit which MAC addresses can connect to a port
To enable VLAN on a port
fcn.expl: Port security restricts the number and specific MAC addresses allowed on a switch port. Violations can cause the port to shutdown, restrict, or protect (discard).
fcn.takeaway: Port security = limits/controls which MAC addresses can use a port

fcn.q: What is the default violation mode for port security on Cisco switches?
fcn.expl: The default violation mode for port security is 'shutdown' - when a violation occurs, the port is placed in error-disabled state and must be manually re-enabled.
fcn.takeaway: Port security default violation = shutdown (port goes err-disabled)

fcn.q: What is 'auto-negotiation' in Ethernet?
opts: Automatic detection and configuration of speed and duplex settings
fcn.expl: Auto-negotiation allows Ethernet devices to automatically negotiate the best common speed (10/100/1000 Mbps) and duplex (half/full) settings on a link.
fcn.takeaway: Auto-negotiation = automatic speed and duplex detection between devices

fcn.q: What causes a 'duplex mismatch' in Ethernet?
opts: Two devices using different IP subnets
One end is full-duplex and the other is half-duplex
Two devices with different VLAN configurations
One device using 802.3 and other using 802.11
fcn.expl: A duplex mismatch occurs when one end is configured for full-duplex and the other is half-duplex. This causes late collisions and poor performance.
fcn.takeaway: Duplex mismatch = one end full-duplex, other half-duplex -> late collisions, poor performance

fcn.q: What is PoE (Power over Ethernet)?
opts: A method to deliver electrical power over Ethernet cables
fcn.expl: PoE (IEEE 802.3af/at/bt) allows Ethernet cables to carry electrical power alongside data, powering devices like IP phones, APs, and cameras without a separate power supply.
fcn.takeaway: PoE = power delivery over Ethernet cable (IP phones, APs, cameras)

fcn.q: What is the purpose of an 'uplink' port on a switch?
opts: A port designed to connect to other switches or routers (upstream devices)
A port for connecting servers only
fcn.expl: Uplink ports are designed to connect switches to other switches or routers. They often support higher speeds or auto-MDI/MDIX for direct connections.
fcn.takeaway: Uplink port = connects switch to other switches or routers (upstream)

fcn.q: What is a VLAN?
opts: A logical grouping of switch ports into separate broadcast domains
fcn.expl: A VLAN (Virtual LAN) is a logical grouping of devices configured on one or more switches that creates separate broadcast domains, regardless of physical location.
fcn.takeaway: VLAN = logical broadcast domain on a switch (independent of physical location)

fcn.q: What is the IEEE standard for VLAN tagging?
fcn.expl: IEEE 802.1Q defines VLAN tagging - adding a 4-byte tag to Ethernet frames to identify which VLAN they belong to on trunk links.
fcn.takeaway: VLAN tagging standard = IEEE 802.1Q (4-byte tag in Ethernet frame)

fcn.q: What is the default VLAN on Cisco switches?
fcn.expl: VLAN 1 is the default VLAN on Cisco switches. All ports are in VLAN 1 by default, and management traffic uses VLAN 1 unless changed. VLAN 1 cannot be deleted.
fcn.takeaway: Default VLAN on Cisco = VLAN 1 (all ports default here; cannot be deleted)

fcn.q: What is the native VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk?
opts: VLAN whose frames are NOT tagged on the trunk
The VLAN assigned to access ports
fcn.expl: The native VLAN is the only VLAN that sends untagged frames on an 802.1Q trunk. Both sides must agree on the native VLAN or a security vulnerability exists.
fcn.takeaway: Native VLAN = untagged VLAN on 802.1Q trunk (default = VLAN 1)

fcn.q: What is VTP (VLAN Trunking Protocol)?
opts: Cisco proprietary protocol to propagate VLAN database across switches
fcn.expl: VTP (VLAN Trunking Protocol) is Cisco proprietary. It synchronizes VLAN information across connected switches. Modes: Server (create/modify VLANs), Client (receive only), Transparent (local only).
fcn.takeaway: VTP = Cisco VLAN sync protocol; modes: Server, Client, Transparent

fcn.q: What is VTP transparent mode?
opts: Forwards VTP advertisements but does not apply them; uses local VLAN config
Sends VTP updates to other switches
fcn.expl: In VTP transparent mode, the switch forwards VTP advertisements to other switches but does not apply them. It uses its own local VLAN database.
fcn.takeaway: VTP transparent = forwards VTP but uses local VLAN config (ignores VTP updates)

fcn.q: What command creates VLAN 10 on a Cisco switch?
fcn.expl: 'vlan [number]' in Global Configuration mode creates a VLAN. Then 'name [name]' optionally names it. This adds it to the VLAN database.
fcn.takeaway: Create VLAN = 'vlan [number]' in global config mode

fcn.q: Which command assigns a switch port to VLAN 10 as an access port?
fcn.expl: Under interface config: 'switchport mode access' sets the port as access, then 'switchport access vlan 10' assigns it to VLAN 10.
fcn.takeaway: Access port VLAN = 'switchport mode access' + 'switchport access vlan 10'

fcn.q: What command configures a trunk port on a Cisco switch?
fcn.expl: 'switchport mode trunk' under interface config configures the port as a permanent trunk. Use 'switchport mode dynamic auto/desirable' for negotiated trunking (DTP).
fcn.takeaway: Configure trunk = 'switchport mode trunk' on interface

fcn.q: What is inter-VLAN routing?
opts: Forwarding traffic between different VLANs using a Layer 3 device
fcn.expl: Inter-VLAN routing forwards traffic between different VLANs. It requires a Layer 3 device (router or Layer 3 switch). Methods: Router-on-a-Stick or SVI on multilayer switch.
fcn.takeaway: Inter-VLAN routing = L3 device routes traffic between different VLANs

fcn.q: What is a 'Router-on-a-Stick'?
opts: A router with only one physical interface using subinterfaces for inter-VLAN routing
A router used as a firewall
A router with multiple WAN connections
A router acting as a DHCP server
fcn.expl: Router-on-a-Stick uses a single trunk link from switch to router with subinterfaces (one per VLAN). Each subinterface has an IP and VLAN encapsulation. Traffic between VLANs routes through it.
fcn.takeaway: Router-on-a-stick = one physical link + subinterfaces for inter-VLAN routing

fcn.q: What is a Switched Virtual Interface (SVI)?
opts: A physical port on a Layer 3 switch
A virtual interface on a multilayer switch for VLAN routing (gateway IP)
A WAN interface on a router
fcn.expl: An SVI (interface VLAN [x]) is a virtual Layer 3 interface on a multilayer switch. It provides the default gateway IP address for devices in that VLAN.
fcn.takeaway: SVI = virtual L3 interface on multilayer switch (acts as VLAN gateway)

fcn.q: VLAN hopping attack exploits which feature?
opts: Native VLAN or DTP auto-trunking to gain unauthorized VLAN access
fcn.expl: VLAN hopping uses DTP auto-negotiation or double-tagging with the native VLAN to access unauthorized VLANs. Prevention: disable DTP, change native VLAN from VLAN 1.
fcn.takeaway: VLAN hopping = exploits DTP auto-trunk or native VLAN double-tagging

fcn.q: What does DTP stand for in Cisco switching?
fcn.expl: DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol) is Cisco's protocol for negotiating trunk links between switches automatically. Security best practice: disable DTP and configure trunks manually.
fcn.takeaway: DTP = Dynamic Trunking Protocol (auto-negotiates trunk; disable for security)

fcn.q: How many VLANs can be configured in the normal range on Cisco switches?
fcn.expl: Normal-range VLANs: 1-1005. Extended-range VLANs: 1006-4094. VLANs 1002-1005 are reserved for Token Ring and FDDI. Maximum total = 4094.
fcn.takeaway: Normal VLANs = 1-1005; Extended = 1006-4094; Reserved = 1002-1005

fcn.q: What is a 'private VLAN'?
opts: A VLAN that cannot be seen by other switches
VLANs that isolate ports within the same VLAN from communicating with each other
A VLAN used only for management
A VLAN encrypted with private keys
fcn.expl: Private VLANs (PVLAN) allow isolation within a VLAN - ports in the same VLAN cannot communicate with each other directly. Used in hotels/hosting where devices in same VLAN need isolation.
fcn.takeaway: Private VLAN = ports in same VLAN isolated from each other (hosting/hotels)

fcn.q: What is the Cisco command to verify VLAN assignments on a switch?
fcn.expl: 'show vlan brief' displays a summary table of all VLANs, their names, status, and which ports are assigned to each VLAN.
fcn.takeaway: show vlan brief = displays VLAN names, status, and port assignments

fcn.q: Voice VLAN is used to:
opts: Segregate IP phone traffic for QoS and security
fcn.expl: Voice VLANs segregate IP phone traffic onto a separate VLAN from data traffic. This enables QoS prioritization for voice, improves security, and simplifies management.
fcn.takeaway: Voice VLAN = separates VoIP traffic for QoS prioritization and security

fcn.q: What happens to frames on the native VLAN when traversing an 802.1Q trunk?
opts: They are tagged with VLAN ID 1
fcn.expl: On an 802.1Q trunk, the native VLAN frames are sent WITHOUT a VLAN tag. This is the only untagged VLAN on a trunk. Both ends must agree on which VLAN is native.
fcn.takeaway: Native VLAN on trunk = frames sent UNTAGGED (no 802.1Q tag)

fcn.q: What is 'VLAN pruning' in VTP?
opts: Restricting trunk links to only carry VLANs that have active members on that link
Splitting large VLANs into smaller ones
fcn.expl: VTP pruning stops a switch from sending VLAN traffic on trunk links to switches that have no ports in that VLAN, reducing unnecessary flooding and bandwidth waste.
fcn.takeaway: VTP pruning = stops sending VLAN traffic to switches with no members in that VLAN

fcn.q: What command sets an IP address on a Layer 2 switch management interface?
fcn.expl: Layer 2 switches do not route. Management IP is set on the SVI: 'interface vlan 1' then 'ip address [IP] [mask]' then 'no shutdown'. Then set 'ip default-gateway'.
fcn.takeaway: Switch management IP = 'interface vlan 1' -> 'ip address [IP] [mask]' -> 'no shutdown'

fcn.q: What does 'spanning-tree portfast' do on a switch port?
opts: Bypasses the listening and learning states for edge ports connecting to end hosts
Disables spanning tree on the port
fcn.expl: PortFast immediately puts a port into forwarding state, bypassing STP listening/learning states. Used ONLY on ports connecting to end hosts (not switches) to avoid 30-second delays.
fcn.takeaway: PortFast = skips STP listening/learning -> instant forwarding (use on host ports only)

fcn.q: What is BPDU Guard used for on switch ports?
opts: Protects PortFast-enabled ports from receiving BPDUs (err-disables port if BPDU received)
fcn.expl: BPDU Guard disables (err-disables) a PortFast port if it receives a BPDU. This prevents attackers from connecting switches to edge ports to disrupt the spanning tree.
fcn.takeaway: BPDU Guard = shuts down port if BPDU received (prevents STP attacks on edge ports)

fcn.q: Which Cisco switch command shows the MAC address table?
fcn.expl: 'show mac address-table' displays the CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table - the learned MAC addresses and their associated switch ports and VLANs.
fcn.takeaway: show mac address-table = displays learned MAC addresses and their ports

fcn.q: What is 'port mirroring' (SPAN) on a switch used for?
opts: Copying traffic from one port to another for monitoring/capture
fcn.expl: SPAN (Switched Port ANalyzer) copies frames from a monitored port/VLAN to a destination port where a network analyzer is connected, enabling traffic capture.
fcn.takeaway: SPAN/Port mirroring = copies traffic to monitor port (for network analysis/IDS)

fcn.q: Which IOS command enables port security on a Cisco switch port?
fcn.expl: 'switchport port-security' under interface configuration enables port security with default settings (max 1 MAC, shutdown violation mode).
fcn.takeaway: Enable port security = 'switchport port-security' (in interface config)

fcn.q: What does the 'sticky' MAC address feature do?
opts: Permanently burns MAC into switch ASIC
Dynamically learns and saves MAC addresses as static port security entries
fcn.expl: 'switchport port-security mac-address sticky' dynamically learns MAC addresses and saves them as static entries in the running config. Save with 'copy run start' to make permanent.
fcn.takeaway: Sticky MAC = auto-learns and stores MACs as static security entries

fcn.q: What does 'switchport trunk allowed vlan' command do?
opts: Restricts which VLANs are allowed on a trunk link
fcn.expl: 'switchport trunk allowed vlan [list]' under interface config restricts the VLANs that are allowed to traverse the trunk link. By default, all VLANs are allowed.
fcn.takeaway: switchport trunk allowed vlan = restricts VLANs on trunk link

fcn.q: To configure SSH access on a Cisco switch, which command generates RSA keys?
fcn.expl: 'crypto key generate rsa' generates the RSA key pair required for SSH. Minimum key size for SSH v2 is 768 bits. Also requires: hostname, domain name, and 'ip ssh version 2'.
fcn.takeaway: Enable SSH = 'crypto key generate rsa' (needs hostname + domain name first)

fcn.q: What Cisco command configures the switch to use SSH version 2?
fcn.expl: 'ip ssh version 2' configures the switch to accept only SSH version 2 connections, which is more secure than version 1.
fcn.takeaway: ip ssh version 2 = restricts SSH to version 2 only (more secure)

fcn.q: What does 'no ip domain-lookup' do on a Cisco device?
opts: Disables DNS resolution on the device
fcn.expl: 'no ip domain-lookup' disables the IOS from trying to resolve mistyped commands as DNS hostnames, which would cause annoying delays while it tries to look them up.
fcn.takeaway: no ip domain-lookup = prevents IOS from DNS-resolving mistyped commands

fcn.q: What is the purpose of 'ip routing' command on a Layer 3 switch?
opts: Enables IP routing capability on a multilayer switch
fcn.expl: 'ip routing' enables the Layer 3 routing capability on a multilayer switch (Layer 3 switch), allowing it to route between VLANs using SVIs instead of relying on an external router.
fcn.takeaway: ip routing = enables Layer 3 routing on a multilayer switch

fcn.q: The Cisco command 'show spanning-tree' displays:
opts: Spanning tree topology, root bridge, port roles/states
fcn.expl: 'show spanning-tree' shows the STP topology: root bridge ID, local bridge ID, and for each port - its role (root/designated/alternate) and state (forwarding/blocking).
fcn.takeaway: show spanning-tree = STP topology, root bridge, port roles and states

fcn.q: What is the maximum number of MAC addresses port security can allow on a port by default?
fcn.expl: The default maximum MAC addresses for port security is 1. When exceeded, a violation occurs. The maximum can be changed with 'switchport port-security maximum [n]'.
fcn.takeaway: Port security default max MACs = 1 (change with 'switchport port-security maximum')

fcn.q: What Cisco command configures interface range fa0/1 through fa0/24 simultaneously?
fcn.expl: 'interface range fa0/1 - 24' (or 'interface range fa0/1-24') selects multiple interfaces at once, allowing the same configuration to be applied to all of them simultaneously.
fcn.takeaway: interface range fa0/1 - 24 = configure multiple interfaces at once

fcn.q: What problem does STP (Spanning Tree Protocol) solve?
opts: Layer 2 switching loops and broadcast storms
fcn.expl: STP prevents Layer 2 loops in Ethernet networks that have redundant paths. Without STP, broadcast storms would bring down the network as frames loop endlessly.
fcn.takeaway: STP = prevents Layer 2 loops and broadcast storms in redundant topologies

fcn.q: Which IEEE standard defines STP?
fcn.expl: IEEE 802.1D defines the original Spanning Tree Protocol (STP). IEEE 802.1W defines RSTP (Rapid STP). IEEE 802.1S defines MSTP.
fcn.takeaway: STP = IEEE 802.1D; RSTP = 802.1W; MSTP = 802.1S

fcn.q: How does STP elect the Root Bridge?
opts: Switch with lowest Bridge ID (priority + MAC address)
Switch configured as root manually always wins
fcn.expl: The Root Bridge is elected by the lowest Bridge ID = Priority (default 32768) + VLAN ID + MAC address. Ties in priority are broken by lowest MAC address.
fcn.takeaway: Root Bridge = lowest Bridge ID = lowest priority + lowest MAC (default priority 32768)

fcn.q: What is the default STP bridge priority on Cisco switches?
fcn.expl: The default STP bridge priority is 32768. To make a switch the root bridge, lower its priority (e.g., 4096 or 0 with 'spanning-tree vlan [x] root primary').
fcn.takeaway: Default STP bridge priority = 32768 (lower = more likely to be root bridge)

fcn.q: What is a Root Port in STP?
opts: The port on the root bridge
The port on a non-root switch with the lowest cost path to the root bridge
The designated port on each segment
fcn.expl: Each non-root switch has exactly ONE Root Port - the port with the lowest-cost path to the Root Bridge. Root ports are always in forwarding state.
fcn.takeaway: Root Port = one per non-root switch; lowest-cost path to Root Bridge (forwarding)

fcn.q: What is a Designated Port in STP?
opts: The port on each segment with the lowest cost toward the root bridge (forwarding)
fcn.expl: Each network segment has exactly one Designated Port - the port with the best path to the Root Bridge on that segment. Designated ports are in forwarding state.
fcn.takeaway: Designated Port = one per segment; best path to root (forwarding state)

fcn.q: What is the STP port state after blocking state?
fcn.expl: STP port states in order: Disabled -> Blocking -> Listening -> Learning -> Forwarding. Listening and Learning each take 15 seconds (forward delay), making convergence 30-50 seconds.
fcn.takeaway: STP states: Disabled->Blocking->Listening(15s)->Learning(15s)->Forwarding

fcn.q: In which STP state does a port populate the MAC address table?
fcn.expl: In the Learning state, the port processes incoming frames and builds the MAC address table but does not forward frames. This prepares the switch for forwarding.
fcn.takeaway: Learning state = builds MAC table but NO forwarding yet

fcn.q: What is RSTP (Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol)?
opts: IEEE 802.1W - faster STP convergence (seconds instead of 30-50 seconds)
fcn.expl: RSTP (IEEE 802.1W) dramatically reduces convergence time from 30-50 seconds to a few seconds using proposal/agreement mechanisms and new port roles.
fcn.takeaway: RSTP (802.1W) = faster STP convergence (seconds vs 30-50 sec)

fcn.q: What is PVST+ in Cisco switching?
opts: Per-VLAN Spanning Tree Plus - separate STP instance per VLAN
fcn.expl: PVST+ (Per-VLAN Spanning Tree Plus) runs a separate STP instance for each VLAN, allowing different root bridges per VLAN for load balancing. Cisco default.
fcn.takeaway: PVST+ = separate STP instance per VLAN (allows load balancing)

fcn.q: STP Hello BPDUs are sent every:
fcn.expl: BPDUs (Bridge Protocol Data Units) are sent every 2 seconds by the Root Bridge. Other timers: Forward Delay = 15s (listening+learning), Max Age = 20s.
fcn.takeaway: STP Hello = 2 seconds; Forward Delay = 15 sec; Max Age = 20 sec

fcn.q: What is the STP path cost of a 100 Mbps link?
fcn.expl: STP path costs: 10 Mbps=100, 100 Mbps=19, 1 Gbps=4, 10 Gbps=2. Lower cost = preferred path. The Root Port is the port with lowest total cost to root.
fcn.takeaway: STP costs: 10Mbps=100, 100Mbps=19, 1Gbps=4, 10Gbps=2

fcn.q: How do you manually set a Cisco switch as the STP root bridge for VLAN 1?
fcn.expl: 'spanning-tree vlan 1 root primary' automatically sets the switch priority to ensure it wins root bridge election. It sets priority to 24576 or lower.
fcn.takeaway: Set STP root = 'spanning-tree vlan 1 root primary' (auto-sets lowest priority)

fcn.q: Which feature protects against BPDUs being received on edge/PortFast ports?
fcn.expl: BPDU Guard is typically enabled on PortFast ports. If a BPDU is received, the port is immediately error-disabled, preventing rogue switches from affecting the STP topology.
fcn.takeaway: BPDU Guard = err-disables port if BPDU received (protects PortFast ports)

fcn.q: Root Guard prevents:
opts: A configured non-root switch from becoming root bridge
BPDUs from propagating to access ports
fcn.expl: Root Guard on a port prevents that port from being used to elect a new root bridge. If a superior BPDU arrives, the port moves to root-inconsistent state (blocks).
fcn.takeaway: Root Guard = prevents port from participating in root bridge election

fcn.q: What is MSTP (Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol)?
opts: Runs one STP instance for all VLANs
Maps multiple VLANs to a limited number of STP instances
fcn.expl: MSTP (IEEE 802.1s) maps multiple VLANs to a smaller number of STP instances, combining PVST+ VLAN-awareness with RSTP speed and reducing CPU overhead.
fcn.takeaway: MSTP (802.1s) = maps multiple VLANs to fewer STP instances (efficiency + RSTP speed)

fcn.q: What does 'loop guard' protect against in STP?
opts: Ports accidentally transitioning to forwarding due to missing BPDUs (unidirectional link failure)
fcn.expl: Loop Guard prevents a non-designated port from transitioning to forwarding if BPDUs stop arriving (unidirectional link failure). Port moves to loop-inconsistent state instead.
fcn.takeaway: Loop Guard = prevents forwarding transition when BPDUs stop (unidirectional failure)

fcn.q: What is 'BackboneFast' in STP?
opts: Speeds up convergence by detecting indirect link failures
Provides load balancing across redundant links
fcn.expl: BackboneFast detects indirect link failures (through inferior BPDUs) and reduces max-age timer delay by 20 seconds, speeding up convergence after an indirect failure.
fcn.takeaway: BackboneFast = detects indirect failures; reduces 20s Max Age delay

fcn.q: Which RSTP port role provides instant failover for root port?
fcn.expl: RSTP Alternate port is a pre-computed backup for the Root Port. When the root port fails, the Alternate port immediately transitions to forwarding without STP delay.
fcn.takeaway: RSTP Alternate port = instant failover backup for Root Port

fcn.q: What is the effect of STP topology change notification (TCN)?
opts: All MAC addresses are flushed to relearn topology
fcn.expl: When a topology change occurs, TCN BPDUs propagate to root, which sends TC BPDUs to all switches. Switches flush MAC tables (age out quickly) to re-learn correct paths.
fcn.takeaway: STP TCN = MAC tables flushed to relearn topology after change

fcn.q: What protocol automatically assigns IP addresses to network clients?
fcn.expl: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically assigns IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS servers to network clients.
fcn.takeaway: DHCP = automatic IP assignment (address, mask, gateway, DNS)

fcn.q: What is the DHCP DORA process?
fcn.expl: DHCP DORA: Discover (client broadcasts), Offer (server responds with IP), Request (client accepts offer), Acknowledge (server confirms). After this, client has IP configuration.
fcn.takeaway: DHCP DORA = Discover -> Offer -> Request -> Acknowledge

fcn.q: What is a DHCP relay agent used for?
opts: To proxy DHCP requests across router boundaries (different subnets)
To provide a backup DHCP server
To convert DHCP to static IP
fcn.expl: A DHCP relay agent (ip helper-address) forwards broadcast DHCP Discover messages across router boundaries so a single DHCP server can serve multiple subnets.
fcn.takeaway: DHCP relay = forwards DHCP broadcasts across routers (ip helper-address)

fcn.q: What is DNS used for in networking?
opts: Resolving domain names to IP addresses
fcn.expl: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (www.example.com) into IP addresses. It uses a hierarchical distributed database.
fcn.takeaway: DNS = translates domain names to IP addresses (hierarchical distributed system)

fcn.q: What is a DNS 'A record'?
fcn.expl: DNS record types: A = IPv4 address, AAAA = IPv6 address, MX = mail server, CNAME = alias, NS = name server, PTR = reverse lookup.
fcn.takeaway: DNS A record = hostname to IPv4 address (AAAA = IPv6)

fcn.q: What protocol is used for remote management using encryption?
fcn.expl: SSH (Secure Shell) provides encrypted remote management. It replaced Telnet which sends all data including passwords in plain text.
fcn.takeaway: SSH = encrypted remote management (replaces insecure Telnet)

fcn.q: What does NTP stand for and what is it used for?
opts: Network Time Protocol - synchronizes clocks across network devices
fcn.expl: NTP (Network Time Protocol) synchronizes the clocks of computers over a network. Accurate time is critical for logging, security certificates, and routing protocols.
fcn.takeaway: NTP = Network Time Protocol (clock synchronization across network devices)

fcn.q: What is TFTP and how does it differ from FTP?
opts: TFTP is TCP-based; FTP is UDP-based
TFTP is simpler, UDP-based, no authentication; FTP is TCP-based with authentication
TFTP is encrypted; FTP is not
TFTP supports directory listing; FTP does not
fcn.expl: TFTP (Trivial FTP) uses UDP port 69 with minimal overhead - no authentication, no directory listing. Used for booting devices and transferring router IOS images.
fcn.takeaway: TFTP = simple file transfer, UDP port 69, no auth (used for IOS upgrades)

fcn.q: What is OSPF's algorithm for finding the shortest path?
fcn.expl: OSPF uses Dijkstra's Shortest Path First algorithm on its link-state database (LSDB) to calculate the best loop-free path to every destination.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF = Dijkstra SPF algorithm on link-state database

fcn.q: What is BGP used for?
opts: Routing within a single autonomous system
Routing between different autonomous systems (Internet routing)
fcn.expl: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the Internet's routing protocol. It routes between different Autonomous Systems (AS) and is used by ISPs and large enterprises.
fcn.takeaway: BGP = Internet's EGP; routes between different Autonomous Systems (ISPs use it)

fcn.q: What is the difference between IGP and EGP?
opts: IGP routes within an AS; EGP routes between AS
IGP uses TCP; EGP uses UDP
IGP is Cisco-only; EGP is open standard
fcn.expl: IGP (Interior Gateway Protocol) routes within an Autonomous System (RIP, OSPF, EIGRP). EGP (Exterior Gateway Protocol) routes between AS. BGP is the only current EGP.
fcn.takeaway: IGP = within AS (RIP, OSPF, EIGRP); EGP = between AS (BGP only)

fcn.q: What is an 'Autonomous System' in networking?
opts: A group of networks under a single administrative domain with a common routing policy
fcn.expl: An Autonomous System (AS) is a collection of networks under a single administrative authority with a unified routing policy, identified by an AS Number (ASN).
fcn.takeaway: Autonomous System = networks under single admin with common routing policy (has ASN)

fcn.q: What is Gratuitous ARP?
opts: An ARP request for your own IP address
An ARP reply sent without a request (for IP conflict detection or IP-MAC update)
An ARP request that crosses router boundaries
fcn.expl: Gratuitous ARP is an ARP reply sent proactively (without a request) announcing a device's own IP-MAC mapping. Used to detect IP conflicts and update other devices' ARP caches.
fcn.takeaway: Gratuitous ARP = unsolicited ARP reply; used for IP conflict detection and cache updates

fcn.q: Which routing protocol uses the Diffusing Update Algorithm (DUAL)?
fcn.expl: EIGRP (Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol) uses Cisco's DUAL algorithm to guarantee loop-free paths and provide fast convergence.
fcn.takeaway: EIGRP uses DUAL algorithm (guarantees loop-free paths, fast convergence)

fcn.q: What does HSRP provide?
opts: Hot Standby Router Protocol - default gateway redundancy
Highly Secure Routing Protocol - encrypted routing
fcn.expl: HSRP (Hot Standby Router Protocol) is Cisco's proprietary default gateway redundancy protocol. It creates a virtual IP/MAC that multiple routers share, providing transparent failover.
fcn.takeaway: HSRP = Cisco default gateway redundancy (virtual IP shared by multiple routers)

fcn.q: What is VRRP?
opts: Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol - open standard default gateway redundancy
fcn.expl: VRRP (Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol - RFC 5798) is the open standard equivalent of Cisco's HSRP. One router is Master, others are Backup, sharing a virtual IP.
fcn.takeaway: VRRP = open standard default gateway redundancy (like HSRP but vendor-neutral)

fcn.q: What is the function of the 'tracert' command in Windows?
opts: Tests if a host is reachable
Displays the path packets take to a destination, showing each hop
fcn.expl: Tracert (Windows) / traceroute (Linux) shows the route packets take to a destination, displaying each router hop, IP address, and round-trip time for each hop.
fcn.takeaway: tracert/traceroute = shows hop-by-hop path to destination

fcn.q: What is 'netstat -an' used for?
opts: Displays all active TCP/UDP connections and listening ports with numeric addresses
fcn.expl: 'netstat -an' shows all active network connections (TCP/UDP) with numeric IP addresses and port numbers, plus all listening ports on the local system.
fcn.takeaway: netstat -an = shows all active connections + listening ports (numeric)

fcn.q: Which command verifies end-to-end IP connectivity?
fcn.expl: Ping (using ICMP Echo Request/Reply) tests basic IP connectivity between source and destination. It verifies the network path is reachable.
fcn.takeaway: ping = tests end-to-end IP connectivity using ICMP Echo Request/Reply

fcn.q: What is the purpose of 'nslookup' command?
opts: Queries DNS servers to resolve domain names or IPs
fcn.expl: nslookup queries DNS servers to resolve domain names to IP addresses (or reverse lookup). Used to troubleshoot DNS issues.
fcn.takeaway: nslookup = queries DNS for name/IP resolution troubleshooting

fcn.q: What class does the IP address 192.168.1.1 belong to?
fcn.expl: Class C addresses range from 192.0.0.0 to 223.255.255.255. 192.168.1.1 is a Class C private address with default mask /24 (255.255.255.0).
fcn.takeaway: 192.168.x.x = Class C (private range 192.168.0.0/16); default mask /24

fcn.q: What is the default subnet mask for a Class B network?
fcn.expl: Class B has a default mask of /16 (255.255.0.0). Class A = /8, Class B = /16, Class C = /24. Class B addresses: 128.0.0.0 to 191.255.255.255.
fcn.takeaway: Class A=/8, Class B=/16, Class C=/24 (default masks)

fcn.q: How many usable host addresses does a /24 network have?
fcn.expl: A /24 network has 8 host bits: 2^8 = 256 addresses total. Subtract 2 (network address and broadcast) = 254 usable hosts.

fcn.q: What is the private IP address range for Class A?
fcn.expl: RFC 1918 private ranges: Class A: 10.0.0.0/8, Class B: 172.16.0.0/12, Class C: 192.168.0.0/16. These are not routable on the public Internet.
fcn.takeaway: Private ranges: Class A=10.0.0.0/8, Class B=172.16.0.0/12, Class C=192.168.0.0/16

fcn.q: What is the APIPA address range?
fcn.expl: APIPA (Automatic Private IP Addressing) assigns addresses in 169.254.0.0/16 when a DHCP server is unreachable. These addresses are link-local only.
fcn.takeaway: APIPA = 169.254.0.0/16 (assigned when DHCP fails; link-local only)

fcn.q: How many subnets does a /27 mask create from a /24 network?
fcn.expl: Moving from /24 to /27 borrows 3 bits (27-24=3). 2^3 = 8 subnets. Each /27 subnet has 32 addresses (30 usable hosts).
fcn.takeaway: /24 to /27 = borrows 3 bits = 8 subnets x 30 hosts each

fcn.q: What is the broadcast address of 192.168.10.64/26?
fcn.expl: /26 = 64 addresses per subnet. 192.168.10.64 network -> broadcast = 192.168.10.64 + 63 = 192.168.10.127. Next subnet starts at .128.

fcn.q: VLSM stands for:
fcn.expl: VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking) allows different subnet masks to be used for different subnets within the same network, making more efficient use of IP addresses.
fcn.takeaway: VLSM = different-sized subnets from same address space (efficient IP usage)

fcn.q: CIDR notation /20 means the subnet mask has how many consecutive 1s?
fcn.expl: CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) /20 notation means the first 20 bits of the address are the network portion. Mask = 255.255.240.0.
fcn.takeaway: CIDR /20 = 20 network bits; mask = 255.255.240.0

fcn.q: What is supernetting (route summarization) in CIDR?
opts: Breaking a network into smaller subnets
Combining multiple consecutive networks into a single routing entry
Assigning multiple IPs to one host
fcn.expl: Supernetting aggregates multiple consecutive networks into a single summary route, reducing routing table size. Example: 192.168.0.0/24 through 192.168.3.0/24 -> 192.168.0.0/22.
fcn.takeaway: Supernetting = combines multiple networks into one routing entry (reduces table size)

fcn.q: What is the network address of 172.16.35.0 with mask 255.255.224.0?
How many hosts can a /30 subnet support?
fcn.expl: /30 has 2 host bits: 2^2=4 addresses. Subtract network+broadcast = 2 usable hosts. /30 is typically used for point-to-point WAN links.
fcn.takeaway: /30 = 2 usable hosts (used for point-to-point WAN links)

fcn.q: What is the correct CIDR notation for subnet mask 255.255.255.240?
fcn.takeaway: 255.255.255.240 = /28 (16 addresses per subnet, 14 usable hosts)

fcn.q: What is the number of usable hosts in a /28 subnet?
fcn.expl: /28 has 4 host bits: 2^4=16 addresses. Subtract 2 (network + broadcast) = 14 usable hosts.

fcn.q: Which of the following is NOT a valid host address in 10.0.0.0/8?
fcn.expl: 10.0.0.0 is the network address (all host bits = 0) - not assignable to hosts. 10.255.255.255 would be the broadcast. All other listed addresses are valid hosts.
fcn.takeaway: Network address (all host bits=0) and broadcast (all host bits=1) are NOT assignable

fcn.q: A company needs 500 hosts. Which subnet mask provides exactly enough space?
fcn.expl: /23 has 9 host bits: 2^9-2 = 510 usable hosts. /24 = 254 (too few). /22 = 1022 (more than needed). /23 is the smallest mask providing >=500 hosts.
fcn.takeaway: /23 = 510 usable hosts (9 host bits: 2^9-2); right size for 500 hosts

fcn.q: FLSM stands for:
fcn.expl: FLSM (Fixed Length Subnet Masking) uses the same subnet mask for all subnets in a network. Simple but wasteful as all subnets are the same size regardless of need.
fcn.takeaway: FLSM = same mask for ALL subnets (simple but wasteful)

fcn.expl: These 4 networks are contiguous. 192.168.4.0 to 192.168.7.255. The common prefix is the first 22 bits. Summary = 192.168.4.0/22.
fcn.takeaway: 4 contiguous /24 networks = summarize to /22 (4 = 2^2 -> borrow 2 bits back)

fcn.q: Which IP address class is used for multicasting?
fcn.expl: Class D addresses (224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255) are reserved for multicast. Class E (240.0.0.0+) is experimental. A, B, C are unicast.

fcn.q: What is the IP address 127.0.0.1 used for?
fcn.expl: 127.0.0.1 (localhost) is the loopback address. Packets sent to this address are processed locally without going onto the network. Used for local testing.
fcn.takeaway: 127.0.0.1 = loopback/localhost (packets stay on local device)

fcn.q: In CIDR, what does the '/prefix' notation represent?
opts: The number of network bits (1s in the subnet mask)
fcn.expl: CIDR prefix notation (e.g., /24) represents the number of consecutive 1-bits in the subnet mask from the left. /24 = 24 ones in the mask = 255.255.255.0.
fcn.takeaway: CIDR /prefix = number of 1-bits in subnet mask (network portion length)

fcn.q: What is the valid host range for 192.168.1.192/26?
fcn.expl: /26: 64 addresses per subnet. Network: .192, Broadcast: .255 (.192+63). Valid hosts: .193 to .254 (network+1 to broadcast-1).

fcn.q: How many bits are in an IPv4 address?
fcn.expl: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits long, written as four octets in dotted-decimal notation. This provides 2^32 = ~4.3 billion possible addresses.
fcn.takeaway: IPv4 = 32 bits (4 octets); 2^32 approximately 4.3 billion addresses

fcn.q: The subnet mask 255.255.252.0 in CIDR notation is:
fcn.takeaway: 255.255.252.0 = /22 (combines 4 Class C networks)

fcn.q: What happens if two devices have the same IP address?
opts: One or both devices experience connectivity issues (IP conflict)
The router automatically assigns a new IP
fcn.expl: Duplicate IP addresses cause IP conflicts - both devices experience intermittent connectivity as ARP responses alternate between them, causing confusion.
fcn.takeaway: Duplicate IP = IP conflict; both devices have intermittent connectivity

fcn.q: What is the purpose of RFC 1918?
opts: Defines private IP address ranges not routable on the Internet
fcn.expl: RFC 1918 defines three ranges of private IP addresses (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x.x) that are not routable on the public Internet and are used for internal networks with NAT.
fcn.takeaway: RFC 1918 = defines private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16.x, 192.168.x) for internal use

fcn.q: You have 192.168.0.0/24 and need 4 equal subnets. Which mask do you use?
fcn.expl: 4 subnets from /24 requires borrowing 2 bits (2^2=4). /24+2 = /26. Each /26 subnet has 64 addresses (62 usable hosts).
fcn.takeaway: 4 equal subnets from /24 = /26 (borrow 2 bits: 2^2=4 subnets, 62 hosts each)

fcn.q: What is the wildcard mask for /24 (255.255.255.0)?
fcn.expl: Wildcard mask = inverse of subnet mask. /24 = 255.255.255.0 -> wildcard = 0.0.0.255. This matches any host in the /24 network.
fcn.takeaway: Wildcard for /24 = 0.0.0.255 (255 - subnet mask octet)

fcn.q: What is the subnet ID of 10.1.1.130/25?
fcn.expl: /25 splits into two halves: 10.1.1.0/25 (.0-.127) and 10.1.1.128/25 (.128-.255). 10.1.1.130 falls in the second subnet: network = 10.1.1.128.

fcn.q: What is a static route?
opts: A route learned automatically by a routing protocol
A manually configured route that does not change unless manually modified
A route that changes based on traffic
A route learned from neighboring routers
fcn.expl: A static route is a manually configured route in the routing table. It does not change automatically and requires manual updates when topology changes occur.
fcn.takeaway: Static route = manually configured; does not auto-update (admin must change it)

fcn.q: What is the Cisco IOS command to add a static route?
fcn.expl: 'ip route [network] [mask] [next-hop IP or exit interface]' adds a static route. Example: ip route 10.0.0.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.1
fcn.takeaway: Static route = 'ip route [network] [mask] [next-hop]'

fcn.q: What is a 'default route' (gateway of last resort)?
opts: Route used only for directly connected networks
Route used when no specific match is found in the routing table
Route used for management traffic only
fcn.expl: A default route (0.0.0.0/0) is the 'gateway of last resort' - used when no more specific route matches the destination. Configured with 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]'.
fcn.takeaway: Default route = 0.0.0.0/0; used when no specific match exists

fcn.q: What is RIP (Routing Information Protocol)?
opts: A distance-vector routing protocol using hop count as metric
fcn.expl: RIP is a distance-vector routing protocol that uses hop count as its metric (max 15 hops). RIP v1 is classful, RIP v2 supports VLSM and authentication.
fcn.takeaway: RIP = distance-vector; metric = hop count; max 15 hops

fcn.q: What is the maximum hop count for RIP?
fcn.expl: RIP's maximum hop count is 15. A hop count of 16 means 'unreachable' (infinity). This severely limits RIP's use in large networks.
fcn.takeaway: RIP max hops = 15 (16 = unreachable/infinity)

fcn.q: What is the administrative distance of RIP?
fcn.expl: Administrative distances: Connected=0, Static=1, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, IS-IS=115, RIP=120, External EIGRP=170, Unknown=255.
fcn.takeaway: RIP AD = 120 (Connected=0, Static=1, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120)

fcn.q: What is RIP's update interval?
fcn.expl: RIP sends full routing table updates every 30 seconds to all RIP neighbors (broadcast for v1, multicast 224.0.0.9 for v2). This wastes bandwidth in large networks.
fcn.takeaway: RIP update interval = 30 seconds (full table broadcast/multicast)

fcn.q: What problem does 'split horizon' solve in distance-vector routing?
fcn.expl: Split horizon prevents routing loops by not advertising a route back out the interface from which it was learned. This is a key loop-prevention mechanism in distance-vector protocols.
fcn.takeaway: Split horizon = do not advertise route back the way it came (prevents loops)

fcn.q: What does 'poison reverse' do in routing?
opts: Advertises failed routes with infinite metric (16 for RIP) back to prevent loops
fcn.expl: Poison reverse advertises failed routes with metric=infinity back to the source, overriding split horizon to ensure immediate loop prevention.
fcn.takeaway: Poison reverse = advertise failed route as infinite metric (overrides split horizon)

fcn.q: What does EIGRP stand for?
fcn.expl: EIGRP (Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol) is Cisco's advanced distance-vector/hybrid protocol using DUAL algorithm for fast, loop-free convergence.
fcn.takeaway: EIGRP = Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (Cisco hybrid protocol)

fcn.q: What metric does EIGRP use?
opts: Composite metric using bandwidth and delay (plus load, reliability, MTU optional)
fcn.expl: EIGRP's metric combines bandwidth (lowest along path) and delay (sum along path). By default: Metric = (10^7/BW + Delay*256) * 256. Load, reliability, MTU are optional.
fcn.takeaway: EIGRP metric = composite (bandwidth + delay by default)

fcn.q: What is the administrative distance of EIGRP?
fcn.expl: EIGRP internal routes have AD=90. EIGRP external routes have AD=170. Compare: OSPF=110, RIP=120. Lower AD = more trusted.
fcn.takeaway: EIGRP internal AD = 90; EIGRP external AD = 170

fcn.q: What is OSPF?
opts: A link-state routing protocol using Dijkstra algorithm
fcn.expl: OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) is an open standard link-state protocol (RFC 2328). Routers exchange LSAs to build an LSDB, then run Dijkstra SPF to find best paths.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF = open standard link-state protocol; uses Dijkstra SPF; RFC 2328

fcn.q: What is the OSPF metric called?
fcn.expl: OSPF uses 'cost' as its metric. Default cost = 10^8 / bandwidth. FastEthernet (100Mbps) = cost 1. T1 (1.544 Mbps) = cost ~64. Lower cost = preferred path.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF metric = cost (10^8/BW); FastEthernet=1, Serial=64

fcn.q: What is the OSPF administrative distance?
fcn.expl: OSPF has an administrative distance of 110 for all OSPF routes (internal and external). EIGRP=90, RIP=120.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF AD = 110

fcn.q: What is an OSPF 'Area'?
opts: A logical grouping of OSPF routers sharing the same LSDB, reducing routing overhead
fcn.expl: OSPF uses areas to limit LSA flooding and SPF calculations. All areas must connect to Area 0 (backbone). Smaller areas = less overhead and faster convergence.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF Area = logical grouping sharing LSDB; Area 0 = backbone (all others connect to it)

fcn.q: What is the OSPF backbone area?
fcn.expl: OSPF Area 0 is the backbone area. All other areas must connect to Area 0, either directly or through virtual links. Inter-area traffic passes through Area 0.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF backbone = Area 0 (all areas must connect here)

fcn.q: What OSPF router type connects Area 0 to other areas?
fcn.expl: An ABR (Area Border Router) has interfaces in multiple OSPF areas, including Area 0. It summarizes routes between areas and generates Type 3 LSAs.
fcn.takeaway: ABR = connects Area 0 to other areas (has interfaces in 2+ areas)

fcn.q: What is the OSPF DR/BDR election?
opts: Designated Router/Backup DR elected on point-to-point links
Designated Router/Backup DR elected on multi-access networks to reduce LSA flooding
A backup for the OSPF backbone
fcn.expl: On multi-access networks (Ethernet), OSPF elects a DR and BDR to reduce LSA flooding. All routers form adjacency with DR/BDR only, not each other.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF DR/BDR = elected on multi-access networks to reduce LSA flooding

fcn.q: What is IGRP?
opts: Interior Gateway Routing Protocol - Cisco distance-vector protocol (predecessor to EIGRP)
fcn.expl: IGRP (Interior Gateway Routing Protocol) was Cisco's proprietary distance-vector protocol using bandwidth and delay as metrics. It is obsolete, replaced by EIGRP.
fcn.takeaway: IGRP = Cisco old distance-vector protocol (obsolete; replaced by EIGRP)

fcn.q: What are the three tables EIGRP maintains?
opts: Neighbor table, Topology table, Routing table
fcn.expl: EIGRP maintains: Neighbor table (adjacent routers), Topology table (all learned routes including DUAL calculations), Routing table (best routes installed).
fcn.takeaway: EIGRP tables = Neighbor + Topology + Routing table

fcn.q: In EIGRP, what is the 'Feasible Successor'?
opts: A backup route that meets the feasibility condition (loop-free alternative path)
The first router to advertise a route
fcn.expl: A Feasible Successor is a backup route whose advertised distance is less than the current Successor's feasible distance - guaranteeing it is loop-free. Provides instant failover.
fcn.takeaway: Feasible Successor = backup EIGRP route (loop-free by feasibility condition = instant failover)

fcn.q: How does OSPF form neighbor relationships?
opts: Exchanging Hello packets on interfaces configured with same area, subnet, timers
Using TCP to establish peer connections
Using manually configured neighbor statements always
fcn.expl: OSPF routers become neighbors by exchanging Hello packets. Requirements: same area, subnet, hello/dead timers, authentication, and stub flag must match.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF neighbors = exchange Hello packets (must match: area, subnet, timers, auth)

fcn.q: What is 'administrative distance' used for?
opts: Determining which routing source to trust when multiple sources know about same destination
fcn.expl: Administrative distance (AD) is a trustworthiness rating for routing information sources. When multiple protocols know about the same network, the one with the lowest AD is installed.
fcn.takeaway: Administrative distance = routing source trustworthiness (lower = more trusted)

fcn.q: What is 'floating static route'?
opts: A static route with a very low AD
A static route with higher AD than a dynamic protocol, used as backup
A dynamic route that behaves like static
A route that changes IP addresses
fcn.expl: A floating static route has a higher AD than the primary dynamic route. When the dynamic route disappears, the floating static takes over as backup.
fcn.takeaway: Floating static = high AD static route; activates when dynamic route disappears

fcn.q: Which command enables RIP on a Cisco router?
fcn.expl: 'router rip' enters RIP configuration mode, then 'network [major network address]' advertises networks. RIP v2 also needs 'version 2'.
fcn.takeaway: Enable RIP = 'router rip' then 'network [network]' (and 'version 2' for RIPv2)

fcn.q: What is RIPv2 improvement over RIPv1?
opts: RIPv2 uses link-state; RIPv1 uses distance-vector
RIPv2 supports VLSM, multicast updates (224.0.0.9), and authentication; RIPv1 is classful, broadcasts
RIPv2 has higher hop count limit
RIPv2 is Cisco-proprietary; RIPv1 is open standard
fcn.expl: RIPv2 improvements over RIPv1: supports VLSM (includes subnet mask in updates), uses multicast (224.0.0.9 vs broadcast), supports MD5 authentication.
fcn.takeaway: RIPv2 vs RIPv1: adds VLSM support, multicast (224.0.0.9), and authentication

fcn.q: What is OSPF Hello and Dead interval default on Ethernet?
fcn.expl: OSPF default timers on Ethernet: Hello = 10 seconds, Dead = 40 seconds (4x hello). On serial/NBMA: Hello = 30s, Dead = 120s. Mismatched timers prevent adjacency.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF Ethernet timers: Hello=10s, Dead=40s (must match on both sides)

fcn.q: What is 'route redistribution' in networking?
opts: Moving routes from one interface to another
Sharing routing information between different routing protocols
fcn.expl: Route redistribution allows routing information to be shared between different routing protocols (e.g., redistributing OSPF routes into EIGRP). Requires careful metrics/AD management.
fcn.takeaway: Route redistribution = sharing routes between different routing protocols

fcn.q: What is 'passive-interface' in routing protocols?
opts: Stops routing updates from being sent/received on that interface (still advertises network)
fcn.expl: 'passive-interface' stops the routing protocol from sending/receiving updates on that interface while still advertising the network. Used to prevent sending updates to end hosts.
fcn.takeaway: passive-interface = no routing updates sent/received (but network still advertised)

fcn.q: What is 'equal-cost multipath' (ECMP) routing?
opts: Using multiple routes with the same metric to load-balance traffic
fcn.expl: ECMP allows a router to install multiple routes with equal metrics to the same destination, load-balancing traffic across multiple paths simultaneously.
fcn.takeaway: ECMP = multiple equal-cost routes installed; load-balances traffic (OSPF, EIGRP support it)

fcn.q: What is OSPF LSA Type 1?
opts: Router LSA - generated by every router describing its directly connected links
fcn.expl: OSPF LSA types: 1=Router (each router, stays within area), 2=Network (DR generates, stays in area), 3=Summary (ABR, inter-area), 4=ASBR Summary, 5=External (ASBR), 7=NSSA External.
fcn.takeaway: OSPF LSA 1=Router, 2=Network(DR), 3=Summary(ABR), 5=External(ASBR)

fcn.q: Which multicast address does OSPF use for DR/BDR communication?
fcn.expl: OSPF uses two multicast addresses: 224.0.0.5 (all OSPF routers) and 224.0.0.6 (all DR/BDR). Non-DR/BDR routers send to 224.0.0.6 to reach DR/BDR. RIP uses 224.0.0.9, EIGRP uses 224.0.0.10.

fcn.q: What is OSPF 'router-id' and how is it determined?
opts: Always the highest IP on the router
Manually configured or auto-selected: highest loopback IP, then highest active interface IP
fcn.expl: OSPF Router-ID selection: 1) Manually configured 'router-id', 2) Highest IP on loopback interfaces, 3) Highest IP on active physical interfaces. Loopback is preferred (always up).
fcn.takeaway: OSPF Router-ID: manual > highest loopback IP > highest active interface IP

fcn.q: What is the OSPF 'default-information originate' command?
opts: Redistributes a default route into OSPF (advertises 0.0.0.0/0 to other routers)
fcn.expl: 'default-information originate' in OSPF config advertises the router's default route (0.0.0.0/0) into the OSPF domain, so other routers know where to send unknown destinations.
fcn.takeaway: default-information originate = advertises default route (0.0.0.0/0) into OSPF

fcn.q: What happens when two OSPF routers have mismatched 'hello' timers?
opts: They still form adjacency but slowly
They form adjacency but with reduced reliability
They cannot form OSPF neighbor relationship
fcn.expl: OSPF requires matching hello and dead timers on both sides of a link. Mismatched timers prevent neighbor formation. Routers must have the same parameters to form adjacency.
